(3) | * [1972](/wiki/1972_Detroit_Tigers_season "1972 Detroit Tigers season") * [1984](/wiki/1984_Detroit_Tigers_season "1984 Detroit Tigers season") * [1987](/wiki/1987_Detroit_Tigers_season "1987 Detroit Tigers season") |

are an American professional [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") team based in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"). The Tigers compete in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB) as a member club of the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) [Central Division](/wiki/American_League_Central "American League Central"). One of the AL's eight charter franchises, the club was founded in Detroit as a member of the minor league [Western League](/wiki/Western_League_(1885%E2%80%931899) "Western League (1885–1899)") in 1894 and is the only Western League team still in its original city. They are also the oldest American League team still playing in their original city under their original name.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

The **Detroit Tigers** are an American professional [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") team based in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"). The Tigers compete in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB) as a member club of the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) [Central Division](/wiki/American_League_Central "American League Central"). One of the AL's eight charter franchises, the club was founded in Detroit as a member of the minor league [Western League](/wiki/Western_League_(1885%E2%80%931899) "Western League (1885–1899)") in 1894 and is the only Western League team still in its original city. They are also the oldest American League team still playing in their original city under their original name.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

Since their establishment as a major league franchise in 1901, the Tigers have won four [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") championships ([1935](/wiki/1935_World_Series "1935 World Series"), [1945](/wiki/1945_World_Series "1945 World Series"), [1968](/wiki/1968_World_Series "1968 World Series"), and [1984](/wiki/1984_World_Series "1984 World Series")), 11 [AL pennants](/wiki/List_of_American_League_pennant_winners "List of American League pennant winners") (1907, 1908, 1909, 1934, 1935, 1940, 1945, 1968, [1984](/wiki/1984_American_League_Championship_Series "1984 American League Championship Series"), [2006](/wiki/2006_American_League_Championship_Series "2006 American League Championship Series"), [2012](/wiki/2012_American_League_Championship_Series "2012 American League Championship Series")), and four AL Central division championships (2011, 2012, 2013 and 2014). They also won division titles in 1972, 1984, and 1987 as a member of the [AL East](/wiki/American_League_East "American League East"). Since [2000](/wiki/2000_Detroit_Tigers_season "2000 Detroit Tigers season"), the Tigers have played their home games at [Comerica Park](/wiki/Comerica_Park "Comerica Park") in [Downtown Detroit](/wiki/Downtown_Detroit "Downtown Detroit").

The Tigers constructed [Bennett Park](/wiki/Bennett_Park_(Detroit) "Bennett Park (Detroit)") at the corner of [Michigan Avenue](/wiki/U.S._Route_12_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 12 in Michigan") and Trumbull Avenue in [Corktown](/wiki/Corktown,_Detroit "Corktown, Detroit") just west of [Downtown Detroit](/wiki/Downtown_Detroit "Downtown Detroit") and began playing there in 1896. In 1912, the team moved into Navin Field, which was built on the same location. It was expanded in 1938 and renamed Briggs Stadium. It was renamed [Tiger Stadium](/wiki/Tiger_Stadium_(Detroit) "Tiger Stadium (Detroit)") in 1961 and the Tigers played there until [1999](/wiki/1999_Detroit_Tigers_season "1999 Detroit Tigers season").

From 1901 to 2025, the Tigers' overall win–loss record is 9,763–9,642–93 (.503).[[10]](#cite_note-12) The franchise's best winning percentage was .656 in [1934](/wiki/1934_Detroit_Tigers_season "1934 Detroit Tigers season"), while its worst was .265 in [2003](/wiki/2003_Detroit_Tigers_season "2003 Detroit Tigers season").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Detroit Tigers](/wiki/History_of_the_Detroit_Tigers "History of the Detroit Tigers")

### Western League beginnings (1894–1900)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Western League beginnings (1894–1900)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Detroit_Tigers_1900.jpg/250px-Detroit_Tigers_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detroit_Tigers_1900.jpg)

1900 Detroit Tigers team photo

The franchise was founded as a member of the reorganized [Western League](/wiki/Western_League_(1885%E2%80%931899) "Western League (1885–1899)") in 1894.[[11]](#cite_note-13) They originally played at Boulevard Park, sometimes called League Park. It was located on East Lafayette, then called Champlain Street, between Helen and East Grand Boulevard, near [Belle Isle](/wiki/Belle_Isle_Park "Belle Isle Park").[[12]](#cite_note-14) In 1895, owner George Vanderbeck decided to build Bennett Park at the corner of Michigan and Trumbull Avenues, which would remain the team's base of operations for the next 104 seasons.[[13]](#cite_note-Bennett_Park-15)[[14]](#cite_note-Dickson-16) The first game at The Corner was an exhibition on April 13, 1896. The team, now occasionally called the "Tigers," beat a local semi-pro team, known as the Athletics, by a score of 30–3.[[13]](#cite_note-Bennett_Park-15)[[14]](#cite_note-Dickson-16) The Tigers played their first Western League game at Bennett Park on April 28, 1896, defeating the [Columbus Senators](/wiki/Columbus_Senators "Columbus Senators") 17–2.[[13]](#cite_note-Bennett_Park-15)[[14]](#cite_note-Dickson-16)

At the end of the 1897 season, [Rube Waddell](/wiki/Rube_Waddell "Rube Waddell") was loaned to the team to gain professional experience. After being fined, Waddell left Detroit to pitch in Canada.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

When the Western League renamed itself the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") for 1900, it was still a minor league, but the next year, it broke from the National Agreement and declared itself a major league, openly competing with the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") for players and for fans in four contested cities.[[16]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-Page_73-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20) For a while, there were rumors of the team relocating to [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"). However, these rumors were put to rest when the two leagues made peace in 1903 when they signed a new National Agreement.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

### First Major League season (1901)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: First Major League season (1901)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/5068_detroit_tigers-cap-1901.png/250px-5068_detroit_tigers-cap-1901.png)](/wiki/File:5068_detroit_tigers-cap-1901.png)

Logo (1901 to 1902)

The Tigers were established as a charter member of the now major league American League in 1901.[[20]](#cite_note-22) They played their first game as a major league team at home against the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/1901_Milwaukee_Brewers_season "1901 Milwaukee Brewers season") on April 25, 1901, with an estimated 10,000 fans at Bennett Park.[[17]](#cite_note-Page_73-19)[[21]](#cite_note-Timeline-23) After entering the ninth inning behind 13–4, the team staged a dramatic comeback to win 14–13.[[21]](#cite_note-Timeline-23) The team finished third in the eight-team league.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

That initial season they were the first major league team to have a mascot—a red tiger on a dark background—on their ballcap. It was replaced by the letter "D" in 1903, and their iconic Olde English-style letterform appeared the following year.[[23]](#cite_note-clair-25)

### The Cobb era (1905–1926)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The Cobb era (1905–1926)")]

#### 1905

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1905")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/1913_Ty_Cobb_portrait_photo.png/250px-1913_Ty_Cobb_portrait_photo.png)](/wiki/File:1913_Ty_Cobb_portrait_photo.png)

[Ty Cobb](/wiki/Ty_Cobb "Ty Cobb") in 1913

In 1905, the team acquired 18-year-old [Ty Cobb](/wiki/Ty_Cobb "Ty Cobb"), a fearless player who came to be regarded as one of the greatest players of all time. The addition of Cobb to an already talented team that included [Sam Crawford](/wiki/Sam_Crawford "Sam Crawford"), [Hughie Jennings](/wiki/Hughie_Jennings "Hughie Jennings"), [Bill Donovan](/wiki/Bill_Donovan "Bill Donovan") and [George Mullin](/wiki/George_Mullin_(baseball) "George Mullin (baseball)") quickly yielded results.[[24]](#cite_note-26)

#### 1907 American League Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1907 American League Champions")]

Main article: [1907 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1907_Detroit_Tigers_season "1907 Detroit Tigers season")

Behind the hitting of outfielders Ty Cobb (.350) and Sam Crawford (.323), and the pitching of Bill Donovan and [Ed Killian](/wiki/Ed_Killian "Ed Killian") (25 wins each), the Tigers went 92–58 to win the AL pennant in 1907 by 1.5 games over the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/1907_Philadelphia_Athletics_season "1907 Philadelphia Athletics season").[[25]](#cite_note-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28) They moved on to [their first World Series appearance](/wiki/1907_World_Series "1907 World Series") against the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/1907_Chicago_Cubs_season "1907 Chicago Cubs season").[[27]](#cite_note-1907_World_Series-29)[[28]](#cite_note-Tie-30)

Game 1 ended in a rare 3–3 tie, called due to darkness after 12 innings.[[27]](#cite_note-1907_World_Series-29)[[28]](#cite_note-Tie-30) The Tigers scored only three runs in the succeeding four games, never scoring more than one run in a game, and lost the Series, 4–0.[[27]](#cite_note-1907_World_Series-29)

#### 1908 American League Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1908 American League Champions")]

Main article: [1908 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1908_Detroit_Tigers_season "1908 Detroit Tigers season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/1908WorldSeries.png/250px-1908WorldSeries.png)](/wiki/File:1908WorldSeries.png)

[1908 World Series](/wiki/1908_World_Series "1908 World Series") program

The Tigers won the AL by just a half-game over the 90–64 [Cleveland Naps](/wiki/1908_Cleveland_Naps_season "1908 Cleveland Naps season") with a 90–63 record.[[29]](#cite_note-31) Cobb hit .324, while Sam Crawford hit .311 with 7 [home runs](/wiki/Home_runs "Home runs"), which was enough to lead the league in the ["dead ball" era](/wiki/Dead-ball_era "Dead-ball era").[[30]](#cite_note-32)

The [Cubs](/wiki/1908_Chicago_Cubs_season "1908 Chicago Cubs season"), however, defeated the Tigers again in the [1908 World Series](/wiki/1908_World_Series "1908 World Series"), this time in five games.[[31]](#cite_note-33) This would be the Cubs' last World Championship until [2016](/wiki/2016_Chicago_Cubs_season "2016 Chicago Cubs season").[[32]](#cite_note-34)

#### 1909 American League Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1909 American League Champions")]

Main article: [1909 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1909_Detroit_Tigers_season "1909 Detroit Tigers season")

In 1909, Detroit posted a 98–54 season, winning the AL pennant by 3.5 games over the [Athletics](/wiki/1909_Philadelphia_Athletics_season "1909 Philadelphia Athletics season").[[33]](#cite_note-35) Ty Cobb won the batting [triple crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_(baseball) "Triple Crown (baseball)") in 1909, hitting .377 with 9 home runs (all inside-the-park) and 107 [RBIs](/wiki/Run_batted_in "Run batted in").[[34]](#cite_note-36)[[35]](#cite_note-1909_AL_batting-37) He also led the league with 76 [stolen bases](/wiki/Stolen_bases "Stolen bases").[[35]](#cite_note-1909_AL_batting-37) George Mullin was the pitching hero, going 29–8 with a 2.22 [ERA](/wiki/Earned_run_average "Earned run average"), while fellow [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") [Ed Willett](/wiki/Ed_Willett "Ed Willett") went 21–10.[[36]](#cite_note-38) Mullin's 11–0 start in 1909 was a Tigers record for 104 years, finally being broken by [Max Scherzer](/wiki/Max_Scherzer "Max Scherzer")'s 13–0 start in [2013](/wiki/2013_Detroit_Tigers_season "2013 Detroit Tigers season").[[37]](#cite_note-39)[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41)

It was hoped that a new opponent in the [1909 Series](/wiki/1909_World_Series "1909 World Series"), the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/1909_Pittsburgh_Pirates_season "1909 Pittsburgh Pirates season"), would yield different results. The Tigers performed better in the Fall Classic, taking Pittsburgh to seven games, but they were blown out 8–0 in the decisive game at Bennett Park.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

#### 1910–1914

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1910–1914")]

The Tigers dropped to third place in the American League in 1910 with an 86–68 record.[[41]](#cite_note-43) They posted 89 wins in 1911 to finish second, but were still well behind a powerhouse [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/1911_Philadelphia_Athletics_season "1911 Philadelphia Athletics season") team that won 101 games.[[42]](#cite_note-44) The team sunk to a dismal sixth place in both the 1912 and 1913 seasons.[[43]](#cite_note-45)[[44]](#cite_note-46) A bright spot in [1912](/wiki/1912_Detroit_Tigers_season "1912 Detroit Tigers season") was George Mullin pitching the franchise's first [no-hitter](/wiki/No-hitter "No-hitter") in a 7–0 win over the [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/1912_St._Louis_Browns_season "1912 St. Louis Browns season") on July 4, his 32nd birthday.[[45]](#cite_note-47)

Cobb went into the stands in a May 15, 1912, game to attack a fan that was abusing him, and was suspended. Three days later, the Tigers protested the suspension by fielding a team of replacement players against the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/1912_Philadelphia_Athletics_season "1912 Philadelphia Athletics season"). They lost 24–2.[[46]](#cite_note-48) During this five-season stretch, Cobb posted [batting averages](/wiki/Batting_average_(baseball) "Batting average (baseball)") of .383, .420, .409, .390 and .368, winning the [batting title](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_batting_champions "List of Major League Baseball batting champions") every year.[[47]](#cite_note-49)

#### 1915

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1915")]

Main article: [1915 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1915_Detroit_Tigers_season "1915 Detroit Tigers season")

In 1915, the Tigers won a then-club record 100 games, but narrowly lost the AL pennant to the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/1915_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1915 Boston Red Sox season"), who won 101 games.[[48]](#cite_note-50) The 1915 Tigers were led by an [outfield](/wiki/Outfield "Outfield") consisting of Ty Cobb, Sam Crawford, and [Bobby Veach](/wiki/Bobby_Veach "Bobby Veach") that finished #1, #2, and #3 in RBIs and [total bases](/wiki/Total_bases "Total bases").[[49]](#cite_note-James-51) Cobb also set a stolen base record with 96 steals in 1915 that stood until 1962, when it was broken by [Maury Wills](/wiki/Maury_Wills "Maury Wills").[[50]](#cite_note-52) Baseball historian [Bill James](/wiki/Bill_James "Bill James") has ranked the 1915 Tigers outfield as the greatest in the history of baseball.[[49]](#cite_note-James-51) The only team in Tigers' history with a better winning percentage than the 1915 squad was the 1934 team that lost the [World Series](/wiki/1934_World_Series "1934 World Series") to the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/1934_St._Louis_Cardinals_season "1934 St. Louis Cardinals season").[[51]](#cite_note-OKennedy-53)

#### 1916–1920

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1916–1920")]

The Tigers dropped to third place in 1916 with an 87–67 record, and would remain mired in the middle of the AL standings the rest of the decade, never winning more than 80 games.[[52]](#cite_note-54)[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58) In the late teens and into the 1920s, Cobb continued to be the marquee player, though he was pushed by budding star [outfielder](/wiki/Outfielder "Outfielder") [Harry Heilmann](/wiki/Harry_Heilmann "Harry Heilmann"), who went on to hit .342 for his career.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

[Hughie Jennings](/wiki/Hughie_Jennings "Hughie Jennings") left the Tigers after the 1920 season, having accumulated 1,131 wins as a [manager](/wiki/Baseball_manager "Baseball manager").[[58]](#cite_note-60) This stood as a Tiger record until 1992, when it was broken by [Sparky Anderson](/wiki/Sparky_Anderson "Sparky Anderson").[[59]](#cite_note-Sparky's_record-61) Cobb himself took over managerial duties in 1921, but during his six years at the helm, the Tigers topped out at 86 wins and never won a pennant.[[60]](#cite_note-Cobb_SABR-62)[[61]](#cite_note-Cobb_VD-63)[[62]](#cite_note-64)

#### 1921

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 1921")]

Main article: [1921 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1921_Detroit_Tigers_season "1921 Detroit Tigers season")

In 1921, the Tigers amassed 1,724 [hits](/wiki/Hit_(baseball) "Hit (baseball)") and a team batting average of .316, the highest team hit total and batting average in AL history.[[63]](#cite_note-65) That year, outfielders Harry Heilmann and Ty Cobb finished #1 and #2 in the American League batting race with batting averages of .394 and .389, respectively.[[64]](#cite_note-66) The downfall of the 1921 Tigers, however, was the absence of good pitching. The team ERA was 4.40.[[65]](#cite_note-67) Without pitching to support the offense, the 1921 Tigers finished in sixth place in the American League at 71–82, 27 games behind the [New York Yankees](/wiki/1921_New_York_Yankees_season "1921 New York Yankees season").[[66]](#cite_note-68)

On August 19, 1921, Cobb collected his 3,000th career hit off [Elmer Myers](/wiki/Elmer_Myers "Elmer Myers") of the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox"). Aged 34 at the time, he is still the youngest player to reach that milestone, also reaching it in the fewest at-bats (8,093).[[67]](#cite_note-SportingNews08061999-69)[[68]](#cite_note-HallofFameCobb3000-70)

#### 1922–1926

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1922–1926")]

The Tigers continued to field good teams during Ty Cobb's tenure as player-manager, finishing as high as second in [1923](/wiki/1923_Detroit_Tigers_season "1923 Detroit Tigers season"), but lack of quality pitching kept them from winning a pennant.[[61]](#cite_note-Cobb_VD-63)[[69]](#cite_note-71) Harry Heilmann hit .403 in 1923, becoming the last AL player to top .400 until [Ted Williams](/wiki/Ted_Williams "Ted Williams") hit .406 in 1941.[[70]](#cite_note-72) In [1925](/wiki/1925_Detroit_Tigers_season "1925 Detroit Tigers season"), Heilmann collected six hits in a season-ending doubleheader to win the batting title, finishing at .393 to [Tris Speaker](/wiki/Tris_Speaker "Tris Speaker")'s .389.[[71]](#cite_note-73)[[72]](#cite_note-74)

Cobb announced his retirement in November 1926 after 22 seasons with the Tigers, though he would return to play two more seasons with the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/History_of_the_Philadelphia_Athletics "History of the Philadelphia Athletics").[[60]](#cite_note-Cobb_SABR-62)[[61]](#cite_note-Cobb_VD-63)

### The Tigers break through (1927–1940)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The Tigers break through (1927–1940)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Hank_Greenberg_1946.jpg/330px-Hank_Greenberg_1946.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hank_Greenberg_1946.jpg)

[Hank Greenberg](/wiki/Hank_Greenberg "Hank Greenberg")

#### 1927–1933

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1927–1933")]

Though the Tigers struggled with mediocre records in the seven years following Cobb's departure, they were building a solid foundation, adding slugging [first baseman](/wiki/First_baseman "First baseman") [Hank Greenberg](/wiki/Hank_Greenberg "Hank Greenberg") and pitchers [Tommy Bridges](/wiki/Tommy_Bridges "Tommy Bridges") and [Schoolboy Rowe](/wiki/Schoolboy_Rowe "Schoolboy Rowe") to a lineup that already included second baseman [Charlie Gehringer](/wiki/Charlie_Gehringer "Charlie Gehringer").[[73]](#cite_note-Cochrane_freep-75)

In [1927](/wiki/1927_Detroit_Tigers_season "1927 Detroit Tigers season"), Harry Heilmann flirted with a .400 batting average all year, eventually finishing at .398 and winning his fourth AL batting title.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

Following the 1933 season, the Tigers added perhaps the final piece of the puzzle, acquiring [catcher](/wiki/Catcher "Catcher") [Mickey Cochrane](/wiki/Mickey_Cochrane "Mickey Cochrane") from the Philadelphia Athletics to serve as player-manager.[[73]](#cite_note-Cochrane_freep-75)

#### 1934 American League Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 1934 American League Champions")]

Main article: [1934 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1934_Detroit_Tigers_season "1934 Detroit Tigers season")

The Tigers won the 1934 AL pennant with a 101–53 record, at the time a team record for wins, and still the best win percentage (.656) in team history.[[51]](#cite_note-OKennedy-53) The Tigers [infield](/wiki/Infield "Infield") (Hank Greenberg and Charlie Gehringer, along with [shortstop](/wiki/Shortstop "Shortstop") [Billy Rogell](/wiki/Billy_Rogell "Billy Rogell") and [third baseman](/wiki/Third_baseman "Third baseman") [Marv Owen](/wiki/Marv_Owen "Marv Owen")) accumulated 462 runs during the season, with Gehringer (214 hits, .356 average) leading the way.[[75]](#cite_note-77)[[76]](#cite_note-78) Schoolboy Rowe led a strong pitching staff, winning 16 straight decisions at one point of the season and finishing with a 24–8 record.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

The Tigers would fall in the [1934 World Series](/wiki/1934_World_Series "1934 World Series") in seven games to the "[Gashouse Gang](/wiki/Gashouse_Gang "Gashouse Gang")" [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/1934_St._Louis_Cardinals_season "1934 St. Louis Cardinals season").[[73]](#cite_note-Cochrane_freep-75)[[78]](#cite_note-1934_World_Series-80) After winning a tight battle in Game 5 with a 3–1 decision over [Dizzy Dean](/wiki/Dizzy_Dean "Dizzy Dean"), Detroit took a 3–2 series lead, but would lose the next two games at Navin Field (Tiger Stadium).[[78]](#cite_note-1934_World_Series-80) For the second time in a World Series Game 7, Detroit folded. St. Louis scored seven times in the third inning off starter [Elden Auker](/wiki/Elden_Auker "Elden Auker") and a pair of relievers, while Dean baffled the Tiger hitters en route to an 11–0 victory.[[73]](#cite_note-Cochrane_freep-75)[[78]](#cite_note-1934_World_Series-80)[[79]](#cite_note-Medwick-81)[[80]](#cite_note-82) The final game was marred by an ugly incident. After spiking Tigers third baseman Marv Owen in the sixth inning, Cardinals left fielder [Joe Medwick](/wiki/Joe_Medwick "Joe Medwick") had to be removed from the game for his own safety by [Commissioner](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Baseball "Commissioner of Baseball") [Kenesaw Mountain Landis](/wiki/Kenesaw_Mountain_Landis "Kenesaw Mountain Landis") after being pelted with debris from angry fans in the large temporary bleacher section in left field.[[73]](#cite_note-Cochrane_freep-75)[[78]](#cite_note-1934_World_Series-80)[[79]](#cite_note-Medwick-81)

#### 1935 World Series Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 1935 World Series Champions")]

Main article: [1935 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1935_Detroit_Tigers_season "1935 Detroit Tigers season")

The Tigers 1935 lineup featured four future Hall of Famers (Hank Greenberg, Mickey Cochrane, [Goose Goslin](/wiki/Goose_Goslin "Goose Goslin") and Charlie Gehringer).[[81]](#cite_note-83) Although they did not challenge the 1934 team's 101 wins, their 93–58 record was good enough to give them the AL pennant by three games over the New York Yankees.[[82]](#cite_note-84) Greenberg was named AL MVP after hitting .328 and leading the league in home runs (36), extra-base hits (98) and RBIs (168).[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-1935_batting-86) Incredibly, Greenberg's RBI total was 48 higher than the next closest player ([Lou Gehrig](/wiki/Lou_Gehrig "Lou Gehrig"), with 120).[[84]](#cite_note-1935_batting-86) The Tigers also got strong contributions from Gehringer (.330), Cochrane (.319) and starting pitchers Tommy Bridges (21–10) and Elden Auker (18–7).[[85]](#cite_note-87)

The Tigers finally won their first [World Series](/wiki/1935_World_Series "1935 World Series"), defeating the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/1935_Chicago_Cubs_season "1935 Chicago Cubs season"), 4–2. Game 6 concluded with Goslin's dramatic walk-off RBI single, scoring Cochrane for a 4–3 victory.[[73]](#cite_note-Cochrane_freep-75)

After owner [Frank Navin](/wiki/Frank_Navin "Frank Navin") died in the offseason, [Walter Briggs Sr.](/wiki/Walter_Briggs_Sr. "Walter Briggs Sr.") took over control of the team.[[86]](#cite_note-88)[[87]](#cite_note-89)

#### 1936–1939

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 1936–1939")]

Despite being forecast to win the American League pennant again in [1936](/wiki/1936_Detroit_Tigers_season "1936 Detroit Tigers season"),[[88]](#cite_note-90) the Tigers fell to a distant second place behind the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") both that season and in [1937](/wiki/1937_Detroit_Tigers_season "1937 Detroit Tigers season").[[89]](#cite_note-91)[[90]](#cite_note-92) The team fell further down the standings with an 84–70 record in [1938](/wiki/1938_Detroit_Tigers_season "1938 Detroit Tigers season") and an 81–73 record in [1939](/wiki/1939_Detroit_Tigers_season "1939 Detroit Tigers season").[[91]](#cite_note-93)[[92]](#cite_note-94) Hank Greenberg nevertheless provided some excitement for Tigers fans in 1938 by challenging the single-season home run record held by [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth") (60). He went into the season's final weekend against the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/1938_Cleveland_Indians_season "1938 Cleveland Indians season") with 58 home runs, tied with [Jimmie Foxx](/wiki/Jimmie_Foxx "Jimmie Foxx") for the most by a right-handed batter at the time, but he failed to homer.[[93]](#cite_note-95)[[94]](#cite_note-96)[[95]](#cite_note-97)

During the final week of the 1938 season, the Tigers presciently held out doubts about a pennant in 1939, but figured that 1940 would be their year.[[96]](#cite_note-98)

#### 1940 American League Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 1940 American League Champions")]

Main article: [1940 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1940_Detroit_Tigers_season "1940 Detroit Tigers season")

In a tight three-team race, the 90–64 Tigers won the 1940 AL pennant by one game over the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/1940_Cleveland_Indians_season "1940 Cleveland Indians season") and two games over the [New York Yankees](/wiki/1940_New_York_Yankees_season "1940 New York Yankees season").[[97]](#cite_note-99) Prior to the season, first baseman Hank Greenberg was persuaded to move to left field to make room for [Rudy York](/wiki/Rudy_York "Rudy York"), whom the Tigers had deemed no longer suitable to be their catcher.[[98]](#cite_note-Greenberg_1940-100) The move proved successful. York hit .316 with 33 home runs and 134 RBIs. Greenberg batted .340 and slammed 41 home runs while driving in 150.[[99]](#cite_note-1940_Tigers_stats-101) Greenberg won his second AL MVP award, becoming the first major leaguer to win the award at two different positions.[[98]](#cite_note-Greenberg_1940-100) Charlie Gehringer batted .313 while collecting 101 walks (for a .428 on-base percentage) and scoring 108 runs.[[99]](#cite_note-1940_Tigers_stats-101)

[Bobo Newsom](/wiki/Bobo_Newsom "Bobo Newsom") was the ace of the Tiger pitching staff in 1940, going 21–5 with a 2.83 ERA.[[99]](#cite_note-1940_Tigers_stats-101) An unlikely hero on the mound this season was 30-year-old rookie [Floyd Giebell](/wiki/Floyd_Giebell "Floyd Giebell"). Making just his third major league start on September 27, Giebell was called upon to pitch the pennant-clinching game against [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller") of the Indians. Feller surrendered just three hits, one being a 2-run homer by Rudy York, while Giebell blanked the Tribe for a 2–0 victory.[[100]](#cite_note-102)[[101]](#cite_note-103)

The Tigers lost the [1940 World Series](/wiki/1940_World_Series "1940 World Series") to the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/1940_Cincinnati_Reds_season "1940 Cincinnati Reds season") in seven games.[[102]](#cite_note-1940_World_Series-104)[[103]](#cite_note-Newson-105) Despite a heroic effort by Bobo Newsom, the Tigers came up short in the deciding game, losing 2–1.[[102]](#cite_note-1940_World_Series-104)[[103]](#cite_note-Newson-105) Newsom's father had died in a Cincinnati hotel room after watching his son win Game 1.[[102]](#cite_note-1940_World_Series-104)[[103]](#cite_note-Newson-105) An inspired Newsom won Game 5 and pitched Game 7 on just one day's rest.[[102]](#cite_note-1940_World_Series-104)[[103]](#cite_note-Newson-105) This was the third time the Tigers had lost a World Series in a deciding seventh game.[[103]](#cite_note-Newson-105)

### The war years (1941–1945)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: The war years (1941–1945)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Hal_Newhouser_Leaf.jpg/250px-Hal_Newhouser_Leaf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hal_Newhouser_Leaf.jpg)

[Hal Newhouser](/wiki/Hal_Newhouser "Hal Newhouser")

With Hank Greenberg serving in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") for all or parts of the 1941–1944 seasons, the Tigers struggled to recapture the glory of 1940.[[104]](#cite_note-106) They finished no higher than fifth place in 1941–1943, but did manage a second-place finish in [1944](/wiki/1944_Detroit_Tigers_season "1944 Detroit Tigers season"), largely on the strength of pitchers [Hal Newhouser](/wiki/Hal_Newhouser "Hal Newhouser") and [Dizzy Trout](/wiki/Dizzy_Trout "Dizzy Trout"), who won 29 and 27 games, respectively.[[105]](#cite_note-107)[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109)[[108]](#cite_note-1944_standings-110)[[109]](#cite_note-1944_Tigers-111) Newhouser, who was 29–9 with a 2.22 ERA, won the first of his two consecutive AL MVP awards this season.[[109]](#cite_note-1944_Tigers-111)[[110]](#cite_note-112) The Tigers were in first place as late as September 18, but would finish one game behind the [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/1944_St._Louis_Browns_season "1944 St. Louis Browns season") for the AL pennant.[[108]](#cite_note-1944_standings-110)[[111]](#cite_note-Blown_lead-113)

#### 1945 World Series Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 1945 World Series Champions")]

Main article: [1945 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1945_Detroit_Tigers_season "1945 Detroit Tigers season")

With the end of World War II and the timely return of Hank Greenberg and others from the military, the Tigers won the AL pennant by just 1.5 games over the [Washington Senators](/wiki/1945_Washington_Senators_season "1945 Washington Senators season") with an 88–65 record.[[112]](#cite_note-114)[[113]](#cite_note-115) [Virgil Trucks](/wiki/Virgil_Trucks "Virgil Trucks") returned from the U.S. Navy in time to pitch 5+1⁄3 innings of 1-run ball in the pennant-clinching game, with starter [Hal Newhouser](/wiki/Hal_Newhouser "Hal Newhouser") pitching the final 3+2⁄3 innings in relief. Greenberg provided the hitting heroics, slugging a go-ahead grand slam homer in the top of the ninth inning.[[114]](#cite_note-116) Newhouser won the [pitching triple crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_(baseball)#Pitching_Triple_Crown "Triple Crown (baseball)"), leading the AL in wins (25), ERA (1.81) and strikeouts (212).[[115]](#cite_note-117) He became the first pitcher in the history of the AL, and still the only pitcher as of 2025, to win the MVP Award in two consecutive seasons.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

With Newhouser, Trucks and Dizzy Trout on the mound and Greenberg leading the offense, Detroit responded in a World Series Game 7 for the first time, staking Newhouser to a 5–0 lead before he threw a pitch en route to a 9–3 victory over the [Cubs](/wiki/1945_Chicago_Cubs_season "1945 Chicago Cubs season").[[117]](#cite_note-119)[[118]](#cite_note-120) Because many stars had not yet returned from the military, some baseball scholars have deemed the [1945 World Series](/wiki/1945_World_Series "1945 World Series") to be among the worst-played contests in World Series history. For example, prior to the World Series, Chicago sportswriter [Warren Brown](/wiki/Warren_Brown_(sportswriter) "Warren Brown (sportswriter)") was asked who he liked, and he answered, "I don't think either one of them can win."[[119]](#cite_note-121)

### A long drought (1946–1967)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: A long drought (1946–1967)")]

#### 1946–1950

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 1946–1950")]

Following their World Series win in 1945, the Tigers continued to have winning records for the remainder of the decade, finishing second in the AL three times, but never winning the pennant.[[120]](#cite_note-122)[[121]](#cite_note-123)[[122]](#cite_note-124)[[123]](#cite_note-125)[[124]](#cite_note-1950_season-126)

Hal Newhouser had another outstanding season in [1946](/wiki/1946_Detroit_Tigers_season "1946 Detroit Tigers season"), again leading the league in wins (26) and ERA (1.94) while striking out a career-high 275 batters. He nearly won his third straight AL MVP award, finishing second to [Ted Williams](/wiki/Ted_Williams "Ted Williams"), who had led the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") to 104 wins (12 games ahead of the second-place Tigers). Also in 1946, the Tigers acquired [George Kell](/wiki/George_Kell "George Kell"), a third baseman who would become a 10-time all-star and Hall of Famer.[[125]](#cite_note-Kell_ESPN-127)[[126]](#cite_note-Kell_NYT-128) He batted over .300 in eight straight seasons (1946–53), and finished with a career .306 average.[[125]](#cite_note-Kell_ESPN-127)[[126]](#cite_note-Kell_NYT-128) Kell won the batting title in a very close race with Ted Williams in [1949](/wiki/1949_Detroit_Tigers_season "1949 Detroit Tigers season"), going 2-for-3 on the last day of the season to edge out the Red Sox slugger, .34291 to .34276.[[125]](#cite_note-Kell_ESPN-127)[[126]](#cite_note-Kell_NYT-128)[[127]](#cite_note-129)

The [1950 season](/wiki/1950_Detroit_Tigers_season "1950 Detroit Tigers season") was particularly frustrating, as the Tigers posted a 95–59 record for a .617 winning percentage, the fourth-best in team history at the time. However, they finished that season three games behind a strong [New York Yankees](/wiki/1950_New_York_Yankees_season "1950 New York Yankees season") team that went on to sweep the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/1950_Philadelphia_Phillies_season "1950 Philadelphia Phillies season") in the [World Series](/wiki/1950_World_Series "1950 World Series").[[124]](#cite_note-1950_season-126)[[128]](#cite_note-130)

#### 1951–1960

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: 1951–1960")]

Over the next 10 years, the Tigers sank to the middle and lower ranks of the American League. The team had only three winning records over this span and never finished higher than fourth place.[[129]](#cite_note-131) The last place [1952 team](/wiki/1952_Detroit_Tigers_season "1952 Detroit Tigers season") went 50–104 (.325), which was the worst season in Tigers history until the [2003 team](/wiki/2003_Detroit_Tigers_season "2003 Detroit Tigers season") lost 119 games.[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-Anderson-133) Despite the dismal season, starter [Virgil Trucks](/wiki/Virgil_Trucks "Virgil Trucks") threw two no-hitters in 1952, becoming only the third pitcher in major league history to accomplish this feat.[[132]](#cite_note-134) 1952 also saw Tiger first baseman [Walt Dropo](/wiki/Walt_Dropo "Walt Dropo") get a hit in 12 consecutive plate appearances over a three-game stretch from July 14 to 15, tying a major league record set by [Johnny Kling](/wiki/Johnny_Kling "Johnny Kling") in 1902.[[133]](#cite_note-135)

Team owner Walter Briggs Sr. died in 1952. His son [Walter Briggs Jr.](/wiki/Walter_Briggs_Jr. "Walter Briggs Jr.") inherited the team, but he was forced to sell it in 1956 to broadcast media owners [John Fetzer](/wiki/John_Fetzer "John Fetzer") and [Fred Knorr](/wiki/Fred_Knorr "Fred Knorr").[[134]](#cite_note-136)[[135]](#cite_note-137)[[136]](#cite_note-138)

Notwithstanding Detroit's fall in the standings, the decade saw the debut of outfielder [Al Kaline](/wiki/Al_Kaline "Al Kaline") in 1953. One of the few major league players who never played a day in the minor leagues, he would hit over .300 nine times in his career. He also made 15 All-Star teams, won 10 Gold Gloves, and featured one of the league's best arms in right field. In 1955, the 20-year-old Kaline hit .340 to become the youngest-ever batting champion in major league history,[[137]](#cite_note-kalinebio-139) finishing runner-up to [Yogi Berra](/wiki/Yogi_Berra "Yogi Berra") in that season's AL MVP voting.[[138]](#cite_note-140)

[1958](/wiki/1958_Detroit_Tigers_season "1958 Detroit Tigers season") saw the Tigers become the second to last team to [integrate their roster](/wiki/Baseball_color_line "Baseball color line") when Dominican player [Ozzie Virgil Sr.](/wiki/Ozzie_Virgil_Sr. "Ozzie Virgil Sr.") joined the team. Only the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") trailed the Tigers in integrating their roster.[[139]](#cite_note-141)[[140]](#cite_note-142)

#### 1961

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: 1961")]

Main article: [1961 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1961_Detroit_Tigers_season "1961 Detroit Tigers season")

As the American League expanded from 8 to 10 teams, Detroit began its slow ascent back to success with an outstanding 1961 campaign. The Tigers led the majors in runs scored and won 101 games, a whopping 30-game improvement over the 71–83 [1960 team](/wiki/1960_Detroit_Tigers_season "1960 Detroit Tigers season"), but still finished eight games behind the [Yankees](/wiki/1961_New_York_Yankees_season "1961 New York Yankees season").[[141]](#cite_note-Alder-143) This marked one of the few times in major league history that a team failed to reach the postseason despite winning 100 or more games, though it had happened once before to the Tigers in 1915.[[141]](#cite_note-Alder-143) First baseman [Norm Cash](/wiki/Norm_Cash "Norm Cash") won the batting title with a .361 average, while teammate Al Kaline finished second.[[141]](#cite_note-Alder-143)[[142]](#cite_note-1961_batting-144) Cash never hit over .286 before or after the 1961 season, and would later say of the accomplishment: "It was a freak. Even at the time, I realized that."[[143]](#cite_note-145) Cash's plate heroics, which also included 41 home runs and 132 RBI, might have earned him MVP honors were it not for New York's [Roger Maris](/wiki/Roger_Maris "Roger Maris") bashing a then record 61 homers the same season.[[141]](#cite_note-Alder-143) Cash also drew 124 walks for a league-leading .487 on-base percentage.[[142]](#cite_note-1961_batting-144) Tigers outfielder [Rocky Colavito](/wiki/Rocky_Colavito "Rocky Colavito") actually bettered Cash's home run and RBI totals, with 45 and 140, respectively.[[144]](#cite_note-146)

The 1961 club featured two non-white starters, [Bill Bruton](/wiki/Bill_Bruton "Bill Bruton") and [Jake Wood](/wiki/Jake_Wood_(baseball) "Jake Wood (baseball)"), and later in the 1960s, black players such as [Willie Horton](/wiki/Willie_Horton_(baseball) "Willie Horton (baseball)"), [Earl Wilson](/wiki/Earl_Wilson_(baseball) "Earl Wilson (baseball)"), and [Gates Brown](/wiki/Gates_Brown "Gates Brown") would contribute to Detroit's rise in the standings.[[145]](#cite_note-147)[[146]](#cite_note-148)[[147]](#cite_note-Horton-149)[[148]](#cite_note-Wilson-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151)

#### 1962–1966

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: 1962–1966")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f7/Al_Kaline_1966.jpg)](/wiki/File:Al_Kaline_1966.jpg)

Hall of Fame member [Al Kaline](/wiki/Al_Kaline "Al Kaline"), nicknamed "Mr. Tiger" (1953–1974), was an 18× All-Star

As a strong nucleus developed, Detroit repeatedly posted winning records throughout the 1960s. In 1963, pitchers [Mickey Lolich](/wiki/Mickey_Lolich "Mickey Lolich") and [Denny McLain](/wiki/Denny_McLain "Denny McLain") entered the rotation.[[150]](#cite_note-Lolich-152)[[151]](#cite_note-153) Outfielders Willie Horton (1963), [Mickey Stanley](/wiki/Mickey_Stanley "Mickey Stanley") (1964) and [Jim Northrup](/wiki/Jim_Northrup_(baseball) "Jim Northrup (baseball)") (1964) would also come aboard around this time.[[147]](#cite_note-Horton-149)[[152]](#cite_note-154)[[153]](#cite_note-Northrup-155)

The team managed a third-place finish during a bizarre 1966 season, in which manager [Chuck Dressen](/wiki/Chuck_Dressen "Chuck Dressen") and acting manager [Bob Swift](/wiki/Bob_Swift "Bob Swift") were both forced to resign their posts because of health problems.[[154]](#cite_note-156)[[155]](#cite_note-Dressen-157)[[156]](#cite_note-Swift-158) Thereafter, [Frank Skaff](/wiki/Frank_Skaff "Frank Skaff") took over the managerial reins until the end of the season.[[157]](#cite_note-159) Both Dressen and Swift died during the year; Dressen died of a heart attack in August, while Swift died of lung cancer in October.[[155]](#cite_note-Dressen-157)[[156]](#cite_note-Swift-158) Following the season, the Tigers hired [Mayo Smith](/wiki/Mayo_Smith "Mayo Smith") to be their new manager.[[158]](#cite_note-160)

#### 1967

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: 1967")]

Main article: [1967 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1967_Detroit_Tigers_season "1967 Detroit Tigers season")

In 1967, the Tigers were involved in one of the closest pennant races in history. Because of rainouts, the Tigers were forced to play back-to-back doubleheaders against the [California Angels](/wiki/1967_California_Angels_season "1967 California Angels season") over the final two days of the season.[[159]](#cite_note-1967_BYB-161)[[160]](#cite_note-1967_SABR-162) They needed to sweep the doubleheader on the last day of the season to force a [one-game playoff](/wiki/One-game_playoff "One-game playoff") with the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/1967_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1967 Boston Red Sox season").[[159]](#cite_note-1967_BYB-161)[[160]](#cite_note-1967_SABR-162) The Tigers won the first game, but lost the second, giving the Red Sox the pennant with no playoff.[[159]](#cite_note-1967_BYB-161)[[160]](#cite_note-1967_SABR-162) Detroit finished the season at 91–71, one game behind Boston.[[161]](#cite_note-163) Starter Earl Wilson, acquired the previous season from the Red Sox, led the Tigers (and the major leagues) with 22 wins and would form a strong 1–2–3 combination with Denny McLain and Mickey Lolich over the next few years.[[148]](#cite_note-Wilson-150)[[162]](#cite_note-164)

### 1968–1972

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: 1968–1972")]

#### Glory in '68: 1968 World Series Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Glory in '68: 1968 World Series Champions")]

Main article: [1968 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1968_Detroit_Tigers_season "1968 Detroit Tigers season")

The Tigers finally returned to the World Series in 1968. The team grabbed first place from the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/1968_Baltimore_Orioles_season "1968 Baltimore Orioles season") on May 10 and would not relinquish the position, clinching the pennant on September 17 and finishing with a 103–59 record.[[163]](#cite_note-165) In a year that was marked by dominant pitching, starter Denny McLain went 31–6 (with a 1.96 ERA), the first time a pitcher had won 30 or more games in a season since Dizzy Dean accomplished the feat in 1934; no pitcher has accomplished it since.[[164]](#cite_note-Herzog-166) McLain won the AL MVP and [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award") for his efforts.[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[166]](#cite_note-168)

##### 1968 World Series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: 1968 World Series")]

Main article: [1968 World Series](/wiki/1968_World_Series "1968 World Series")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/1968_World_Series_program_and_tickets.jpg/250px-1968_World_Series_program_and_tickets.jpg)](/wiki/File:1968_World_Series_program_and_tickets.jpg)

1968 World Series program and tickets for Games 4 and 5 at [Tiger Stadium](/wiki/Tiger_Stadium_(Detroit) "Tiger Stadium (Detroit)")

In the 1968 World Series, the Tigers met the defending World Series champion [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/1968_St._Louis_Cardinals_season "1968 St. Louis Cardinals season"), led by starter [Bob Gibson](/wiki/Bob_Gibson "Bob Gibson"), who had posted a modern-era record 1.12 ERA during the regular season, and speedy outfielder [Lou Brock](/wiki/Lou_Brock "Lou Brock").[[164]](#cite_note-Herzog-166)[[167]](#cite_note-169)[[168]](#cite_note-170) This was the first time the Tigers and Cardinals had met in the World Series since 1934.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) The series was predicated with a bold decision by manager Mayo Smith to play center fielder Mickey Stanley at shortstop, replacing the slick fielding but weak hitting of [Ray Oyler](/wiki/Ray_Oyler "Ray Oyler").[[170]](#cite_note-Hall_SI-172)[[171]](#cite_note-Decisions-173) Stanley had never played shortstop before, but was a Gold Glover in the outfield and an excellent athlete.[[170]](#cite_note-Hall_SI-172) Smith played him at short for the final nine games of the regular season and all seven World Series games, with Oyler only appearing as a late-inning defensive replacement.[[170]](#cite_note-Hall_SI-172)[[171]](#cite_note-Decisions-173) This allowed Smith to play an outfield of Willie Horton, Jim Northrup and Al Kaline in every game.[[170]](#cite_note-Hall_SI-172)[[171]](#cite_note-Decisions-173)

In Game 1, Gibson completely shut down the Detroit lineup, striking out a World Series record 17 batters en route to an easy 4–0 win.[[172]](#cite_note-174)[[173]](#cite_note-175) However, due in no small part to pitcher Mickey Lolich's victories in Games 2 and 5, the Tigers climbed back into the World Series.[[174]](#cite_note-1968_Freep-176) Many fans believe the turning point came in the fifth inning of Game 5, with the Tigers down three games to one, and trailing in the game, 3–2. Left fielder Willie Horton made a perfect throw to home plate to nail Lou Brock, who tried to score from second base standing up, as catcher [Bill Freehan](/wiki/Bill_Freehan "Bill Freehan") blocked the plate with his foot.[[174]](#cite_note-1968_Freep-176)[[175]](#cite_note-Paul-177) The Tigers came back with three runs in the seventh to win that game, 5–3, and stay alive.[[174]](#cite_note-1968_Freep-176)[[175]](#cite_note-Paul-177) The Cardinals would not threaten to score the rest of this game, and scored only two more meaningless runs over the remainder of the series. In Game 6, McLain ensured a Game 7 by notching his only win of the World Series, a 13–1 blowout, despite pitching on only two days' rest.[[174]](#cite_note-1968_Freep-176)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Mickey_Lolich_1975.jpg/250px-Mickey_Lolich_1975.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mickey_Lolich_1975.jpg)

[Mickey Lolich](/wiki/Mickey_Lolich "Mickey Lolich") was the 1968 World Series MVP

In Game 7 at [Busch Memorial Stadium](/wiki/Busch_Memorial_Stadium "Busch Memorial Stadium"), Lolich, also pitching on two days' rest, faced Gibson.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) Both men pitched brilliantly, putting zeros up on the scoreboard for much of the game.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) In the bottom of the sixth inning, the Cardinals looked primed to take the lead as Lou Brock singled to lead off the inning, only to be promptly picked off by Lolich.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) One out later, [Curt Flood](/wiki/Curt_Flood "Curt Flood") followed with another single, and was also picked off by Lolich.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) In the top of the seventh, an exhausted Gibson finally cracked, giving up two-out singles to Norm Cash and Willie Horton.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) Jim Northrup then struck the decisive blow, lashing a triple to center field over the head of Flood, who appeared to misjudge how hard the ball was hit.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) That scored both Cash and Horton; Northrup himself was then brought home by a Bill Freehan double.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) Detroit added an insurance run in the ninth.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) A ninth-inning solo home run by [Mike Shannon](/wiki/Mike_Shannon "Mike Shannon") was all the Cardinals could muster against Lolich as the Tigers took the game, 4–1, and the World Series, 4–3.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171)[[174]](#cite_note-1968_Freep-176)

The Tigers became only the third team to ever win the World Series after being down 3–1.[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171) For his three victories that propelled the Tigers to the championship, Lolich was named the [World Series Most Valuable Player](/wiki/World_Series_MVP_Award "World Series MVP Award").[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171)[[174]](#cite_note-1968_Freep-176) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit), Lolich is the last pitcher to have three complete-game victories in a single World Series.[[150]](#cite_note-Lolich-152)[[169]](#cite_note-1968_Game_7-171)

#### 1969–1971

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: 1969–1971")]

1969 saw further expansion as both leagues realigned into two divisions of six teams, and the Tigers were placed in the [American League East](/wiki/American_League_East "American League East").[[176]](#cite_note-178)[[177]](#cite_note-1969_AL-179) That year, [Detroit](/wiki/1969_Detroit_Tigers_season "1969 Detroit Tigers season") failed to defend its title, despite Denny McLain having another outstanding season with a 24–9 campaign, earning him his second straight Cy Young Award (co-winner with Baltimore's [Mike Cuellar](/wiki/Mike_Cuellar "Mike Cuellar")).[[178]](#cite_note-180) The Tigers' 90 wins placed them a distant second in the division to a very strong [Baltimore Orioles team](/wiki/1969_Baltimore_Orioles_season "1969 Baltimore Orioles season"), which had won 109 games.[[177]](#cite_note-1969_AL-179)[[179]](#cite_note-181)

The Tigers suffered a disappointing 1970 season, finishing fourth in the AL East with a 79–83 record.[[180]](#cite_note-182) Following the season, Mayo Smith was let go and was replaced by [Billy Martin](/wiki/Billy_Martin "Billy Martin").[[181]](#cite_note-183)[[182]](#cite_note-184)[[183]](#cite_note-Martin-185) In a playing career that was primarily spent with the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"), Martin played his final games with the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins") and stayed in that organization after his retirement.[[183]](#cite_note-Martin-185) He managed the Twins to an AL West Division title in [1969](/wiki/1969_Minnesota_Twins_season "1969 Minnesota Twins season"), but was fired after that season due to rocky relationships with his players, which included a legendary fight with pitcher [Dave Boswell](/wiki/Dave_Boswell_(baseball) "Dave Boswell (baseball)") in an alley behind Detroit's Lindell AC sports bar.[[183]](#cite_note-Martin-185)[[184]](#cite_note-186)

Also during the offseason, Denny McLain, who had been suspended three times and had a 3–5 record, was part of an eight-player deal with the [Washington Senators](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball)#Washington_Senators_(1961–1971) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") in what would turn out to be a heist for Detroit.[[185]](#cite_note-1970_trade-187)[[186]](#cite_note-Acocella-188)[[187]](#cite_note-McLain_trade-189)[[188]](#cite_note-Maddox-190) The Tigers acquired pitcher [Joe Coleman](/wiki/Joe_Coleman_(1970s_pitcher) "Joe Coleman (1970s pitcher)"), shortstop [Eddie Brinkman](/wiki/Eddie_Brinkman "Eddie Brinkman") and third baseman [Aurelio Rodríguez](/wiki/Aurelio_Rodr%C3%ADguez "Aurelio Rodríguez").[[185]](#cite_note-1970_trade-187)[[187]](#cite_note-McLain_trade-189)[[188]](#cite_note-Maddox-190)

Martin's [Tigers](/wiki/1971_Detroit_Tigers_season "1971 Detroit Tigers season") posted 91 wins in 1971. However, they had to settle for a second-place finish behind the Orioles, who won 101 games to take their third straight AL East Division crown.[[189]](#cite_note-191) The season was highlighted by Mickey Lolich's 308 strikeouts, which led the AL and is still the single-season record in franchise history.[[150]](#cite_note-Lolich-152) Lolich also won 25 games and posted a 2.92 ERA while throwing an incredible 376 innings and completing 29 of his 45 starts.[[190]](#cite_note-1971_Tigers-192)[[191]](#cite_note-193) Coleman paid immediate dividends for Detroit, winning 20 games,[[190]](#cite_note-1971_Tigers-192) while McLain went 10–22 for the Senators and was out of baseball by the following season.[[186]](#cite_note-Acocella-188)[[187]](#cite_note-McLain_trade-189)[[192]](#cite_note-194)

#### 1972 AL East Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: 1972 AL East Champions")]

Main article: [1972 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1972_Detroit_Tigers_season "1972 Detroit Tigers season")

Joe Coleman, Eddie Brinkman and Aurelio Rodríguez all played critical roles in 1972, when the Tigers captured their first AL East division title.[[187]](#cite_note-McLain_trade-189)[[193]](#cite_note-Quirk-195) Oddities of the schedule due to an early season strike allowed the 86–70 Tigers to win the division by just 1⁄2 game.[[193]](#cite_note-Quirk-195)[[194]](#cite_note-1972_Detroit_Tigers_Statistics-196) Brinkman was named Tiger of the Year by the Detroit Baseball Writers, despite a .203 batting average, as he committed just 7 errors in 728 chances (.990 fielding percentage). He also had a streak of 72 games and 331 chances without an error during the season, both AL records for a shortstop.[[195]](#cite_note-197) Mickey Lolich was his steady self for the Tigers, winning 22 games with a sparkling 2.50 ERA, while Coleman won 19 and had a 2.80 ERA.[[194]](#cite_note-1972_Detroit_Tigers_Statistics-196) Starter [Woodie Fryman](/wiki/Woodie_Fryman "Woodie Fryman"), acquired on August 2, was the final piece of the puzzle as he went 10–3 over the last two months of the regular season and posted a minuscule 2.06 ERA.[[196]](#cite_note-Fryman-198) Fryman was also the winning pitcher in the division-clinching game against the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox"), a 3–1 victory on October 3.[[196]](#cite_note-Fryman-198)[[197]](#cite_note-199)

##### 1972 ALCS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: 1972 ALCS")]

Main article: [1972 American League Championship Series](/wiki/1972_American_League_Championship_Series "1972 American League Championship Series")

In the 1972 American League Championship Series, Detroit faced the American League West division champion [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/1972_Oakland_Athletics_season "1972 Oakland Athletics season"), who had become steadily competitive ever since the 1969 realignment.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200) In Game 1 of the ALCS in Oakland, Mickey Lolich, the hero of '68, took the hill and allowed just one run over nine innings. The Athletics' ace, [Catfish Hunter](/wiki/Catfish_Hunter "Catfish Hunter"), matched Lolich, surrendering only a solo home run to Norm Cash, and the game went into extra innings.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200) Al Kaline hit a solo homer to break a 1–1 tie in the top of the 11th inning, only to be charged with a throwing error on [Gonzalo Márquez](/wiki/Gonzalo_M%C3%A1rquez "Gonzalo Márquez")'s game-tying single in the bottom half of the frame that allowed [Gene Tenace](/wiki/Gene_Tenace "Gene Tenace") to score the winning run.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200) [Blue Moon Odom](/wiki/Blue_Moon_Odom "Blue Moon Odom") shut down Detroit 5–0 in Game 2.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200) The end of Game 2 was marred by an ugly incident in which Tigers reliever [Lerrin LaGrow](/wiki/Lerrin_LaGrow "Lerrin LaGrow") hit A's shortstop and leadoff hitter [Bert Campaneris](/wiki/Bert_Campaneris "Bert Campaneris") on the ankle with a pitch. An angered Campaneris threw the bat at LaGrow, and LaGrow ducked just in time for the bat to sail over his head. Both benches cleared, and though no punches were thrown, both LaGrow and Campaneris were suspended for the remainder of the series. It was widely believed that Martin had ordered the pitch that hit Campaneris, who had three hits, two stolen bases and two runs scored in the game.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200)

As the series shifted to Detroit, the Tigers caught their stride. Joe Coleman held the A's scoreless on seven hits in Game 3, striking out 14 batters in a 3–0 victory for the Tigers.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200)[[199]](#cite_note-201) Game 4 was another pitchers' duel between Hunter and Lolich, resulting again in a 1–1 tie at the end of nine innings. Oakland scored two runs in the top of the 10th and put the Tigers down to their last three outs.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200) Detroit pushed two runs across the plate to tie the game before Jim Northrup came through in the clutch again. His single off [Dave Hamilton](/wiki/Dave_Hamilton_(baseball) "Dave Hamilton (baseball)") scored [Gates Brown](/wiki/Gates_Brown "Gates Brown") to give the Tigers a 4–3 win and even the series at two games apiece.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200)

A first-inning run on an RBI ground out from Bill Freehan, set up by a Gene Tenace passed ball that allowed [Dick McAuliffe](/wiki/Dick_McAuliffe "Dick McAuliffe") to reach third, gave Detroit an early lead in the deciding fifth and final game in Detroit.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200) [Reggie Jackson](/wiki/Reggie_Jackson "Reggie Jackson")'s steal of home in the second inning tied it up, though Jackson was injured in a collision with Freehan and had to leave the game.[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200) Tenace's two-out single to left field plated [George Hendrick](/wiki/George_Hendrick "George Hendrick") to give Oakland a 2–1 lead in the fourth inning. The run was controversial to many Tigers fans, as Hendrick was ruled safe at first base two batters prior to the Tenace hit. Hendrick appeared to be out by two steps on a grounder to short, but umpire John Rice ruled that Norm Cash pulled his foot off first base. Replays and photos, however, show that Cash did not pull his foot.[[153]](#cite_note-Northrup-155)[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200) Thanks to that play and four innings of scoreless relief from [Vida Blue](/wiki/Vida_Blue "Vida Blue"), the A's took the AL pennant and a spot in the [World Series](/wiki/1972_World_Series "1972 World Series").[[198]](#cite_note-1972_ALCS-200)

### A slow decline (1973–1978)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: A slow decline (1973–1978)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Willie_Horton_1975.jpg/250px-Willie_Horton_1975.jpg)](/wiki/File:Willie_Horton_1975.jpg)

[Willie Horton](/wiki/Willie_Horton_(baseball) "Willie Horton (baseball)") (1963–1977) had his No. 23 retired by the club

The [1973 season](/wiki/1973_Detroit_Tigers_season "1973 Detroit Tigers season") saw the Tigers drop to third place in the division, with an 85–77 record.[[200]](#cite_note-202) Joe Coleman posted another 23 wins, but the other Tiger starters had subpar seasons.[[201]](#cite_note-203) Willie Horton hit .316, but injuries limited him to just 111 games.[[147]](#cite_note-Horton-149) Jim Northrup posted the best batting average of his career (.307) but was inexplicably limited to part-time duty (119 games played), which Northrup attributed to an ongoing feud with Billy Martin that had actually started in the 1972 ALCS. Northrup even proclaimed to the press that Martin "took the fun out of the game."[[153]](#cite_note-Northrup-155) Martin did not survive the 1973 season as manager. He was fired that September after ordering his pitchers to throw [spitballs](/wiki/Spitball "Spitball") (and telling the press that he did so) in protest of opposing [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians "Cleveland Indians") pitcher [Gaylord Perry](/wiki/Gaylord_Perry "Gaylord Perry"), whom Martin was convinced was doing the same.[[183]](#cite_note-Martin-185)[[202]](#cite_note-Martin_fired-204) Third base coach [Joe Schultz](/wiki/Joe_Schultz "Joe Schultz") served as interim manager for the remainder of the season.[[202]](#cite_note-Martin_fired-204)

A bright spot for the Tigers in 1973 was relief pitcher [John Hiller](/wiki/John_Hiller "John Hiller"), who marked his first full season since suffering a heart attack in 1971 by collecting a league-leading 38 saves and posting a brilliant 1.44 ERA.[[203]](#cite_note-Hiller-205) Hiller's saves total would stand as a Tiger record until [2000](/wiki/2000_Detroit_Tigers_season "2000 Detroit Tigers season"), when it was broken by [Todd Jones](/wiki/Todd_Jones "Todd Jones")' 42 saves (Jones' record would later be broken by [José Valverde](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Valverde "José Valverde")'s 49 saves in 2011).[[203]](#cite_note-Hiller-205)[[204]](#cite_note-206)[[205]](#cite_note-Valverde_delivery-207)

After the season, the Tigers hired [Ralph Houk](/wiki/Ralph_Houk "Ralph Houk") to be their new manager. Houk served in that capacity for five full seasons, through the end of the 1978 season.[[206]](#cite_note-208) The roster of players who played under Houk were mostly aging veterans from the 1960s, whose performance had slipped from their peak years.[[207]](#cite_note-209) The Tigers did not have a winning season from 1974 to 1977, and their 57 wins in the [1975 season](/wiki/1975_Detroit_Tigers_season "1975 Detroit Tigers season") was the team's lowest since [1952](/wiki/1952_Detroit_Tigers_season "1952 Detroit Tigers season").[[208]](#cite_note-210)[[209]](#cite_note-211)[[210]](#cite_note-1976_season-212)[[211]](#cite_note-213) Perhaps the biggest signal of decline for the Tigers was the retirement of Kaline following the 1974 season, after he notched his [3,000th career hit](/wiki/3,000_hit_club "3,000 hit club").[[212]](#cite_note-214)[[213]](#cite_note-215) Kaline finished with 3,007 hits and was elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame in his first year of eligibility in 1980.[[214]](#cite_note-Kaline_HOF-216)[[215]](#cite_note-217)

#### 1976: The Year of The Bird

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: 1976: The Year of The Bird")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Mark_Fidrych.JPG/250px-Mark_Fidrych.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mark_Fidrych.JPG)

[Mark "The Bird" Fidrych](/wiki/Mark_Fidrych "Mark Fidrych"), 1976 AL Rookie of the Year

Main article: [1976 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1976_Detroit_Tigers_season "1976 Detroit Tigers season")

Tiger fans were provided a glimmer of hope when 21-year-old rookie [Mark Fidrych](/wiki/Mark_Fidrych "Mark Fidrych") made his debut in 1976.[[216]](#cite_note-Fidrych-218) Fidrych, known as "The Bird", was a colorful character known for talking to the baseball and other eccentricities.[[216]](#cite_note-Fidrych-218) During a game against the [Yankees](/wiki/1976_New_York_Yankees_season "1976 New York Yankees season"), [Graig Nettles](/wiki/Graig_Nettles "Graig Nettles") responded to Fidrych's antics by talking to his bat. After making an out, he later lamented that his Japanese-made bat did not understand him.[[217]](#cite_note-219) Fidrych entered the All-Star break at 9–2 with a 1.78 ERA, and was the starting pitcher for the American League in the [All-Star Game](/wiki/1976_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "1976 Major League Baseball All-Star Game") played that year in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Veterans_Stadium "Veterans Stadium") to celebrate the American [Bicentennial](/wiki/United_States_Bicentennial "United States Bicentennial").[[216]](#cite_note-Fidrych-218) He finished the season with a record of 19–9 and an American League-leading [ERA](/wiki/Earned_run_average "Earned run average") of 2.34.[[216]](#cite_note-Fidrych-218) Fidrych, the AL Rookie of the Year, was one of the few bright spots that year with the Tigers finishing next to last in the AL East in 1976.[[210]](#cite_note-1976_season-212)[[216]](#cite_note-Fidrych-218)

Aurelio Rodríguez won the [Gold Glove Award](/wiki/Gold_Glove_Award "Gold Glove Award") in 1976 at third base, snapping a streak in which Hall of Famer [Brooks Robinson](/wiki/Brooks_Robinson "Brooks Robinson") had won it for 16 consecutive seasons.[[218]](#cite_note-220)

#### 1977–1978

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: 1977–1978")]

Injuries to his knee, and later his arm, drastically limited Fidrych's appearances in 1977–78.[[216]](#cite_note-Fidrych-218) Perhaps more important, however, was the talent coming up through the Tigers farm system at the time. [Jack Morris](/wiki/Jack_Morris "Jack Morris"), [Lance Parrish](/wiki/Lance_Parrish "Lance Parrish"), [Alan Trammell](/wiki/Alan_Trammell "Alan Trammell") and [Lou Whitaker](/wiki/Lou_Whitaker "Lou Whitaker") all made their debuts in 1977, and would help the team to 88 wins in [1978](/wiki/1978_Detroit_Tigers_season "1978 Detroit Tigers season"), the only winning season under Houk.[[219]](#cite_note-221)[[220]](#cite_note-222)

### The "Bless You Boys" era (1979–1987)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: The \"Bless You Boys\" era (1979–1987)")]

Houk's immediate successor as Tigers manager in [1979](/wiki/1979_Detroit_Tigers_season "1979 Detroit Tigers season") was [Les Moss](/wiki/Les_Moss "Les Moss"), but Moss would only last until June of that year.[[221]](#cite_note-223) From June 14, 1979, until the end of the 1995 season, the team was managed by [George "Sparky" Anderson](/wiki/Sparky_Anderson "Sparky Anderson"), one of baseball's winningest managers and winner of two [World Series rings](/wiki/World_Series_ring "World Series ring") as manager of the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") during their peak as [The Big Red Machine](/wiki/The_Big_Red_Machine "The Big Red Machine").[[222]](#cite_note-224) When Anderson joined the Tigers in 1979 and assessed the team's young talent, he boldly predicted that it would be a pennant winner within five years.[[223]](#cite_note-225)

Acerbic Detroit TV sports anchor Al Ackerman initiated the phrase "Bless You Boys". Originally used as a sarcastic remark, Ackerman's phrase would take on a new meaning in 1984.[[224]](#cite_note-Ackerman_BYB-226)[[225]](#cite_note-Ackerman_COD-227)

#### The Roar of '84: 1984 World Series Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: The Roar of '84: 1984 World Series Champions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Sparky_Anderson.jpg/250px-Sparky_Anderson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sparky_Anderson.jpg)

[Sparky Anderson](/wiki/Sparky_Anderson "Sparky Anderson") was the manager of the Tigers from 1979 to 1995

Main article: [1984 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1984_Detroit_Tigers_season "1984 Detroit Tigers season")

As in 1968, the Tigers' next World Series season would be preceded by a disappointing second-place finish, as the [1983 Tigers](/wiki/1983_Detroit_Tigers_season "1983 Detroit Tigers season") won 92 games to finish six games behind the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/1983_Baltimore_Orioles_season "1983 Baltimore Orioles season") in the AL East.[[226]](#cite_note-228)

The first major news of the 1984 season actually came in late 1983, when broadcasting magnate John Fetzer, who had owned the Tigers since 1957, sold the team to [Domino's Pizza](/wiki/Domino%27s_Pizza "Domino's Pizza") founder and CEO [Tom Monaghan](/wiki/Tom_Monaghan "Tom Monaghan") for $53 million.[[227]](#cite_note-229)[[228]](#cite_note-230)

The 1984 team got off to a 9–0 start highlighted by [Jack Morris](/wiki/Jack_Morris "Jack Morris") tossing a nationally televised no-hitter against [Chicago](/wiki/1984_Chicago_White_Sox_season "1984 Chicago White Sox season") in the fourth game of the season.[[229]](#cite_note-231)[[230]](#cite_note-232) They stayed hot for most of the year, posting a 35–5 record over their first forty games and cruising to a franchise-record 104 victories.[[231]](#cite_note-DeCamp-233) The Tigers led the division from opening day until the end of the regular season and finished a staggering 15 games ahead of the second-place [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/1984_Toronto_Blue_Jays_season "1984 Toronto Blue Jays season").[[231]](#cite_note-DeCamp-233) Closer [Willie Hernández](/wiki/Willie_Hern%C3%A1ndez "Willie Hernández"), acquired from the 1983 NL champion [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") in the offseason, won both the Cy Young Award and AL MVP, a rarity for a relief pitcher.[[232]](#cite_note-234)

##### 1984 ALCS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: 1984 ALCS")]

Main article: [1984 American League Championship Series](/wiki/1984_American_League_Championship_Series "1984 American League Championship Series")

The Tigers faced the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/1984_Kansas_City_Royals_season "1984 Kansas City Royals season") in the American League Championship Series. In Game 1, Alan Trammell, Lance Parrish and [Larry Herndon](/wiki/Larry_Herndon "Larry Herndon") went deep to crush the Royals 8–1 at Royals Stadium (now [Kauffman Stadium](/wiki/Kauffman_Stadium "Kauffman Stadium")).[[233]](#cite_note-235) In Game 2, the Tigers scored twice in the 11th inning when [Johnny Grubb](/wiki/Johnny_Grubb "Johnny Grubb") doubled off Royals closer [Dan Quisenberry](/wiki/Dan_Quisenberry "Dan Quisenberry") en route to a 5–3 victory.[[234]](#cite_note-Lantz-236)[[235]](#cite_note-237) The Tigers completed the sweep at Tiger Stadium in Game 3. [Marty Castillo](/wiki/Marty_Castillo "Marty Castillo")'s third-inning RBI fielder's choice would be all the help Detroit would need. [Milt Wilcox](/wiki/Milt_Wilcox "Milt Wilcox") outdueled [Charlie Leibrandt](/wiki/Charlie_Leibrandt "Charlie Leibrandt"), and after Hernandez got [Darryl Motley](/wiki/Darryl_Motley "Darryl Motley") to pop out to preserve the 1–0 win, the Tigers were returning to the World Series.[[234]](#cite_note-Lantz-236)[[236]](#cite_note-238)

##### 1984 World Series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: 1984 World Series")]

Main article: [1984 World Series](/wiki/1984_World_Series "1984 World Series")

In the [NLCS](/wiki/1984_National_League_Championship_Series "1984 National League Championship Series"), the [San Diego Padres](/wiki/1984_San_Diego_Padres_season "1984 San Diego Padres season") rallied from losing the first two games to overcome the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/1984_Chicago_Cubs_season "1984 Chicago Cubs season") and prevent a fifth Cubs-Tigers series. The Tigers would open the [1984 World Series](/wiki/1984_World_Series "1984 World Series") on the road in San Diego.[[237]](#cite_note-239)

In Game 1, Larry Herndon hit a two-run home run that gave the Tigers a 3–2 lead.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240) Jack Morris pitched a complete game with 2 runs on 8 hits, and Detroit drew first blood.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[239]](#cite_note-241) The Padres evened the series the next night despite pitcher [Ed Whitson](/wiki/Ed_Whitson "Ed Whitson") being chased after pitching 2⁄3 of an inning and giving up three runs on five Tiger hits.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[240]](#cite_note-1984_WS_Game_2-242) Tigers starter [Dan Petry](/wiki/Dan_Petry "Dan Petry") exited the game after 4+1⁄3 innings when [Kurt Bevacqua](/wiki/Kurt_Bevacqua "Kurt Bevacqua")'s three-run homer gave San Diego a 5–3 lead they would not relinquish.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[240]](#cite_note-1984_WS_Game_2-242)

When the series shifted to the Motor City, the Tigers took command. In Game 3, a two-out rally in the second inning, highlighted by Marty Castillo's 2-run homer, led to four runs and the yanking of Padres starter [Tim Lollar](/wiki/Tim_Lollar "Tim Lollar") after 1+2⁄3 innings.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[241]](#cite_note-1984_WS_Game_3-243) The Padres never recovered, losing 5–2.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[241]](#cite_note-1984_WS_Game_3-243) [Eric Show](/wiki/Eric_Show "Eric Show") continued the parade of bad outings in Game 4, getting bounced after 2+2⁄3 innings after giving up a pair of 2-run homers to World Series MVP Alan Trammell in his first two at-bats. Trammell's homers held up with the help of another Morris complete game, and the Tigers' 4–2 win gave them a commanding lead in the series.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[242]](#cite_note-244)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6e/Kirk_Gibson_83Tigers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kirk_Gibson_83Tigers.jpg)

[Kirk Gibson](/wiki/Kirk_Gibson "Kirk Gibson"), a [Michigan State](/wiki/Michigan_State_University "Michigan State University") alumni, hit the clinching home run in Game 5 of the 1984 World Series

In Game 5, [Kirk Gibson](/wiki/Kirk_Gibson "Kirk Gibson")'s two-run shot in the first inning would be the beginning of another early end for the Padres' starter [Mark Thurmond](/wiki/Mark_Thurmond "Mark Thurmond").[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[243]](#cite_note-1984_WS_Game_5-245)[[244]](#cite_note-Wulf-246) Although the Padres would pull back even at 3–3, chasing Petry in the fourth inning in the process, the Tigers retook the lead on a [Rusty Kuntz](/wiki/Rusty_Kuntz "Rusty Kuntz") sacrifice fly (actually a pop-out to retreating second baseman [Alan Wiggins](/wiki/Alan_Wiggins "Alan Wiggins") that the speedy Gibson was able to score on), and then went up 5–3 on a solo homer by Parrish.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[243]](#cite_note-1984_WS_Game_5-245)[[244]](#cite_note-Wulf-246)

Gibson came to bat in the eighth inning with runners on second and third and the Tigers clinging to a 5–4 lead.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[243]](#cite_note-1984_WS_Game_5-245)[[244]](#cite_note-Wulf-246) A "Sounds of the Game" video made during the Series by MLB Productions captured this moment, and has been played on TV a number of times since then. Padres manager [Dick Williams](/wiki/Dick_Williams "Dick Williams") was shown in the dugout flashing four fingers, ordering an intentional walk, before San Diego reliever [Goose Gossage](/wiki/Goose_Gossage "Goose Gossage") summoned him to the mound. Sparky Anderson was seen and heard yelling to Gibson, "He don't want to walk you!", and making a swing-the-bat gesture.[[245]](#cite_note-247) As Anderson had suspected, Gossage threw a 1–0 fastball on the inside corner, and Gibson was ready. He launched the pitch into Tiger Stadium's right field upper deck for a three-run homer, giving the Tigers a four-run lead and effectively clinching the game and the series.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[243]](#cite_note-1984_WS_Game_5-245)[[244]](#cite_note-Wulf-246)[[246]](#cite_note-248)

[Aurelio López](/wiki/Aurelio_L%C3%B3pez "Aurelio López") pitched 2+1⁄3 innings of relief and retired all seven batters he faced, earning the win. Despite allowing a rare run in the top of the 8th inning, Willie Hernández got the save as [Tony Gwynn](/wiki/Tony_Gwynn "Tony Gwynn") flew out to Larry Herndon to end the game, sending Detroit into a wild victory celebration.[[238]](#cite_note-1984_World_Series-240)[[243]](#cite_note-1984_WS_Game_5-245)[[244]](#cite_note-Wulf-246)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Alan_Trammell.jpg/330px-Alan_Trammell.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alan_Trammell.jpg)

1984 World Series MVP, [Alan Trammell](/wiki/Alan_Trammell "Alan Trammell") (SS)

The Tigers led their division wire-to-wire, from opening day and every day thereafter, culminating in the World Series championship. This had not been done in the major leagues since the [1955 Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/1955_Brooklyn_Dodgers_season "1955 Brooklyn Dodgers season").[[247]](#cite_note-249) With the win, Anderson became the first manager to win the World Series in both leagues.[[248]](#cite_note-250)

#### 1987 AL East Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: 1987 AL East Champions")]

Main article: [1987 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/1987_Detroit_Tigers_season "1987 Detroit Tigers season")

After a pair of third-place finishes in 1985 and 1986,[[249]](#cite_note-251)[[250]](#cite_note-252) the 1987 Tigers faced lowered expectations – which seemed to be confirmed by an 11–19 start to the season.[[251]](#cite_note-Duke-253) However, the team hit its stride thereafter and gradually gained ground on its AL East rivals.[[251]](#cite_note-Duke-253) This charge was fueled in part by the acquisition of pitcher [Doyle Alexander](/wiki/Doyle_Alexander "Doyle Alexander") from the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") in exchange for minor league pitcher [John Smoltz](/wiki/John_Smoltz "John Smoltz"). Alexander started 11 games for the Tigers, posting a 9–0 record and a 1.53 ERA. Smoltz, a Michigan native, went on to have a long and productive career, mostly with the Braves, and was ultimately inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in 2015.[[251]](#cite_note-Duke-253)[[252]](#cite_note-254) Despite the Tigers' great season, they entered September neck-and-neck with the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/1987_Toronto_Blue_Jays_season "1987 Toronto Blue Jays season"). The two teams would square off in seven hard-fought games during the final two weeks of the season. All seven games were decided by one run, and in the first six of the seven games, the winning run was scored in the final inning of play. At [Exhibition Stadium](/wiki/Exhibition_Stadium "Exhibition Stadium"), the Tigers [dropped](/wiki/List_of_baseball_jargon_(D)#drop "List of baseball jargon (D)") three in a row to the Blue Jays before winning a dramatic extra-inning showdown.[[251]](#cite_note-Duke-253)
