The Tigers entered the final week of the 1987 season 2.5 games behind.[[251]](#cite_note-Duke-253) After a series against the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/1987_Baltimore_Orioles_season "1987 Baltimore Orioles season"), the Tigers returned home trailing by a game and swept the Blue Jays.[[251]](#cite_note-Duke-253) Detroit clinched the division in a 1–0 victory over Toronto in front of 51,000 fans at Tiger Stadium on October 4. [Frank Tanana](/wiki/Frank_Tanana "Frank Tanana") went all nine innings for the complete-game shutout, and outfielder Larry Herndon gave the Tigers their lone run on a second-inning home run. Detroit finished the season two games ahead of Toronto, securing the best record in the majors (98–64).[[251]](#cite_note-Duke-253)[[253]](#cite_note-1987_standings-255)

In what would prove to be their last postseason appearance until 2006, the Tigers were upset in the [1987 American League Championship Series](/wiki/1987_American_League_Championship_Series "1987 American League Championship Series") by the 85–77 [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/1987_Minnesota_Twins_season "1987 Minnesota Twins season") (who in turn won the World Series that year) 4–1.[[251]](#cite_note-Duke-253)[[253]](#cite_note-1987_standings-255)[[254]](#cite_note-256) The Twins clinched the series in Game 5 at Tiger Stadium, 9–5.[[255]](#cite_note-257)

### A new approach (1988–1995)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: A new approach (1988–1995)")]

Despite their 1987 division title victory, the [Tigers](/wiki/1988_Detroit_Tigers_season "1988 Detroit Tigers season") proved unable to build on their success. The team lost Kirk Gibson to free agency in the offseason, but still spent much of 1988 in first place in the AL East. A late season slump left the team in second place at 88–74, one game behind the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/1988_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1988 Boston Red Sox season").[[256]](#cite_note-258)[[257]](#cite_note-259)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Cecil_Fielder_1996.jpg/250px-Cecil_Fielder_1996.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cecil_Fielder_1996.jpg)

[Cecil Fielder](/wiki/Cecil_Fielder "Cecil Fielder") in 1996

In 1989, the [team](/wiki/1989_Detroit_Tigers_season "1989 Detroit Tigers season") collapsed to a 59–103 record, worst in the majors.[[258]](#cite_note-260)[[259]](#cite_note-261) The franchise then attempted to rebuild using a power-hitting approach, with sluggers [Cecil Fielder](/wiki/Cecil_Fielder "Cecil Fielder"), [Rob Deer](/wiki/Rob_Deer "Rob Deer") and [Mickey Tettleton](/wiki/Mickey_Tettleton "Mickey Tettleton") joining Trammell and Whitaker in the lineup (fitting for the team with the most 200+ home run seasons in baseball history).[[260]](#cite_note-262) In 1990, Fielder led the American League with 51 home runs (becoming the first player to hit 50 since [George Foster](/wiki/George_Foster_(baseball) "George Foster (baseball)") in 1977, and the first AL player since [Roger Maris](/wiki/Roger_Maris "Roger Maris") and [Mickey Mantle](/wiki/Mickey_Mantle "Mickey Mantle") in 1961), and finished second in the voting for AL MVP.[[261]](#cite_note-263)[[262]](#cite_note-264)[[263]](#cite_note-265) He hit 44 home runs and collected 132 RBI in 1991, again finishing second in the AL MVP balloting.[[264]](#cite_note-266) Behind the hitting of Fielder and others, the Tigers improved by 20 wins in 1990 (79–83), and posted a winning record in 1991 (84–78).[[265]](#cite_note-267)[[266]](#cite_note-268) However, the team lacked quality pitching, despite [Bill Gullickson](/wiki/Bill_Gullickson "Bill Gullickson")'s 20 wins in 1991, and its core of key players began to age, setting the franchise up for decline.[[267]](#cite_note-269) Their minor league system was largely barren of talent as well, producing only a few everyday players during the 1990s.[[268]](#cite_note-270)[[269]](#cite_note-271)[[270]](#cite_note-272) Adding insult to injury, the Tigers and radio station [WJR](/wiki/WJR "WJR") announced in December 1990 that they were not renewing the contract of long-time Hall of Fame play-by play announcer [Ernie Harwell](/wiki/Ernie_Harwell "Ernie Harwell"), and that the 1991 season would be Harwell's last with the team.[[271]](#cite_note-273) The announcement was met with resounding protests from fans, both in Michigan and around the baseball world.[[272]](#cite_note-274)

[1992](/wiki/1992_Detroit_Tigers_season "1992 Detroit Tigers season") saw the Tigers win only 75 games, with Fielder being one of the few bright spots as he won the AL RBI title for a third straight season (124).[[273]](#cite_note-275)[[274]](#cite_note-276) In August 1992, the franchise was sold to [Mike Ilitch](/wiki/Mike_Ilitch "Mike Ilitch"), the President and CEO of [Little Caesars Pizza](/wiki/Little_Caesars "Little Caesars") who also owned the [Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings").[[275]](#cite_note-277) One of Ilitch's first moves as the new owner was to rehire Ernie Harwell.[[276]](#cite_note-278) Late in the season, Sparky Anderson won his 1,132nd game as a Tiger manager, passing Hughie Jennings for the most all-time wins in franchise history.[[59]](#cite_note-Sparky's_record-61) The team also responded with an 85–77 season in [1993](/wiki/1993_Detroit_Tigers_season "1993 Detroit Tigers season"), but it would be their last winning season for a number of years.[[277]](#cite_note-Fangraphs-279)[[278]](#cite_note-Leyland-280)

On October 2, 1995, manager Sparky Anderson chose to not only end his career with the Tigers, but retire from baseball altogether.[[279]](#cite_note-281)

### Randy Smith era (1996–2002)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Randy Smith era (1996–2002)")]

From 1994 to 2005, the Tigers did not post a winning record, the longest sub-.500 stretch in franchise history.[[277]](#cite_note-Fangraphs-279)[[278]](#cite_note-Leyland-280)[[280]](#cite_note-Tigers_HC-282) In 1996, the Tigers lost a then-team record 109 games, under new general manager [Randy Smith](/wiki/Randy_Smith_(baseball) "Randy Smith (baseball)").[[280]](#cite_note-Tigers_HC-282)[[281]](#cite_note-283)[[282]](#cite_note-284) The only team in the majors to have a longer stretch without a winning season during this time were the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates"), who did not have a winning record in the years spanning 1993 to 2012.[[283]](#cite_note-285) The Tigers' best record over this span was 79–83, recorded in 1997 and 2000.[[280]](#cite_note-Tigers_HC-282)

In 1998, the Tigers moved from the AL East, where they had been since the divisions were created in 1969, to the AL Central as part of a realignment necessitated by the addition of the expansion [Tampa Bay Devil Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Devil_Rays "Tampa Bay Devil Rays").[[284]](#cite_note-286)[[285]](#cite_note-287)[[286]](#cite_note-288)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/ComericaParkWEntrance.jpg/250px-ComericaParkWEntrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:ComericaParkWEntrance.jpg)

The entrance sign of Comerica Park

In 2000, the team left [Tiger Stadium](/wiki/Tiger_Stadium_(Detroit) "Tiger Stadium (Detroit)") in favor of [Comerica Park](/wiki/Comerica_Park "Comerica Park").[[287]](#cite_note-289)

Soon after it opened, Comerica Park drew criticism for its deep dimensions, which made it difficult to hit home runs; the distance to left-center field (395 ft), in particular, was seen as unfair to hitters. This led to the nickname "Comerica National Park."[[288]](#cite_note-290) The team made a successful bid to bring in slugger [Juan González](/wiki/Juan_Gonz%C3%A1lez_(baseball) "Juan González (baseball)") from the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") for the inaugural season at Comerica Park.[[289]](#cite_note-291) After four consecutive seasons of no fewer than 39 home runs, González only hit 22 homers in 2000. He cited Comerica Park's dimensions as a major reason why he turned down a multiyear contract extension.[[290]](#cite_note-292) In 2003, the franchise largely quieted the criticism by moving in the left-center fence to 370 feet (110 m), taking the flagpole in that area out of play, a feature carried over from Tiger Stadium.[[291]](#cite_note-293) In 2005, the team moved the bullpens to the vacant area beyond the left field fence and filled the previous location with seats.[[292]](#cite_note-294)

In late 2001, [Dave Dombrowski](/wiki/Dave_Dombrowski "Dave Dombrowski"), former general manager of the [1997 World Series](/wiki/1997_World_Series "1997 World Series") champion [Florida Marlins](/wiki/1997_Florida_Marlins_season "1997 Florida Marlins season"), was hired as team president.[[293]](#cite_note-295) In 2002, the [Tigers](/wiki/2002_Detroit_Tigers_season "2002 Detroit Tigers season") started the [season](/wiki/2002_Major_League_Baseball_season "2002 Major League Baseball season") 0–6, prompting Dombrowski to fire the unpopular Smith, as well as manager [Phil Garner](/wiki/Phil_Garner "Phil Garner"). Dombrowski then took over as general manager and named bench coach [Luis Pujols](/wiki/Luis_Pujols "Luis Pujols") to finish the season as interim manager.[[294]](#cite_note-296)[[295]](#cite_note-297) The team finished 55–106. After the season was over, Pujols was let go.[[296]](#cite_note-298)

### Most losses in franchise history (2003)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Most losses in franchise history (2003)")]

Main article: [2003 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2003_Detroit_Tigers_season "2003 Detroit Tigers season")

Dave Dombrowski hired popular former shortstop Alan Trammell to manage the team in 2003.[[297]](#cite_note-299) With fellow 1984 teammates Kirk Gibson and Lance Parrish on the coaching staff, the rebuilding process began.[[131]](#cite_note-Anderson-133) On August 30, 2003, the Tigers' defeat at the hands of the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/2003_Chicago_White_Sox_season "2003 Chicago White Sox season") caused them to join the [1962 New York Mets](/wiki/1962_New_York_Mets_season "1962 New York Mets season"), who were a first-year expansion team, as the only modern MLB teams to lose 100 games before September.[[298]](#cite_note-300) They avoided tying the 1962 Mets' then modern MLB record of 120 losses only by winning five of their last six games of the season, including three out of four against the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/2003_Minnesota_Twins_season "2003 Minnesota Twins season"), who had already clinched the AL Central and were resting their stars.[[131]](#cite_note-Anderson-133)[[299]](#cite_note-Lapointe-301)

[Mike Maroth](/wiki/Mike_Maroth "Mike Maroth") went 9–21, becoming the first pitcher to lose 20 games since [Brian Kingman](/wiki/Brian_Kingman "Brian Kingman") lost 20 for the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics") in 1980.[[300]](#cite_note-302) Maroth, [Jeremy Bonderman](/wiki/Jeremy_Bonderman "Jeremy Bonderman") (6–19), and [Nate Cornejo](/wiki/Nate_Cornejo "Nate Cornejo") (6–17) were the top three pitchers in losses in the entire major leagues, the first time in history that this had occurred.[[301]](#cite_note-Pressley-303)[[302]](#cite_note-Kornacki-304)[[c]](#cite_note-306)

The Tigers finished 43–119, the worst record in franchise history.[[131]](#cite_note-Anderson-133)[[299]](#cite_note-Lapointe-301)[[302]](#cite_note-Kornacki-304) This eclipsed the previous AL record of 117 losses set by the [1916 Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/1916_Philadelphia_Athletics_season "1916 Philadelphia Athletics season"),[[304]](#cite_note-307) which was later surpassed by the [2024 Chicago White Sox](/wiki/2024_Chicago_White_Sox_season "2024 Chicago White Sox season").[[305]](#cite_note-2024_WS_ESPN-308)[[306]](#cite_note-2024_WS_NBC-309)[[307]](#cite_note-Sharkey-Gotlieb-310) While the 2003 Tigers rank as the fourth worst team in major league history based on total losses,[[305]](#cite_note-2024_WS_ESPN-308)[[307]](#cite_note-Sharkey-Gotlieb-310) they fare slightly better based on winning percentage. Their .265 win percentage is the majors' seventh-worst since 1900.[[301]](#cite_note-Pressley-303)[[307]](#cite_note-Sharkey-Gotlieb-310)

Further information: [List of worst Major League Baseball season records § Modern era (1900–present)](/wiki/List_of_worst_Major_League_Baseball_season_records#Modern_era_(1900–present) "List of worst Major League Baseball season records")

### Rebuilding the franchise (2004–2006)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Rebuilding the franchise (2004–2006)")]

After the embarrassing 2003 season, the Tigers vowed to make changes.[[299]](#cite_note-Lapointe-301)[[308]](#cite_note-311) Under Dave Dombrowski, the franchise demonstrated a willingness to sign marquee free agents.[[309]](#cite_note-312) In 2004, the team signed or traded for several talented but high-risk veterans, such as [Fernando Viña](/wiki/Fernando_Vi%C3%B1a "Fernando Viña"), [Rondell White](/wiki/Rondell_White "Rondell White"), [Iván Rodríguez](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_Rodr%C3%ADguez "Iván Rodríguez"), [Ugueth Urbina](/wiki/Ugueth_Urbina "Ugueth Urbina"), and [Carlos Guillén](/wiki/Carlos_Guill%C3%A9n "Carlos Guillén"), and the gamble paid off.[[310]](#cite_note-313)[[311]](#cite_note-314)[[312]](#cite_note-315)[[313]](#cite_note-316)[[314]](#cite_note-317) The 2004 Tigers finished 72–90, a 29-game improvement over the previous season.[[131]](#cite_note-Anderson-133)[[315]](#cite_note-318)[[316]](#cite_note-Trammell_fired-319) This was the largest improvement in the AL since the Baltimore Orioles had a 33-game improvement from 1988 to 1989.[[316]](#cite_note-Trammell_fired-319)

Prior to the 2005 season, the Tigers spent a large sum for two prized free agents, [Troy Percival](/wiki/Troy_Percival "Troy Percival") and [Magglio Ordóñez](/wiki/Magglio_Ord%C3%B3%C3%B1ez "Magglio Ordóñez").[[317]](#cite_note-320)[[318]](#cite_note-321) On June 8, 2005, the Tigers traded pitcher Ugueth Urbina and infielder [Ramón Martínez](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_Mart%C3%ADnez_(infielder) "Ramón Martínez (infielder)") to the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") for [Plácido Polanco](/wiki/Pl%C3%A1cido_Polanco "Plácido Polanco").[[319]](#cite_note-322) The Tigers stayed on the fringes of contention for the AL [wild card](/wiki/Wild_card_(sports) "Wild card (sports)") for the first four months of the season, but then faded badly, finishing 71–91.[[316]](#cite_note-Trammell_fired-319)[[320]](#cite_note-323) The collapse was perceived as being due both to injuries and to a lack of player unity; Rodríguez in particular was disgruntled, taking a leave of absence during the season to deal with a difficult divorce.[[316]](#cite_note-Trammell_fired-319)[[321]](#cite_note-324) Trammell, though popular with the fans, took part of the blame for the poor clubhouse atmosphere and lack of continued improvement, and he was fired at the end of the season.[[316]](#cite_note-Trammell_fired-319)

A highlight of the 2005 campaign was Detroit's hosting of the [Major League Baseball All-Star Game](/wiki/2005_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "2005 Major League Baseball All-Star Game"), its first since 1971.[[322]](#cite_note-325) In the [Home Run Derby](/wiki/Home_run_derby#2005 "Home run derby"), Rodríguez finished second, losing to the Phillies' [Bobby Abreu](/wiki/Bobby_Abreu "Bobby Abreu").[[323]](#cite_note-326)

In October 2005, [Jim Leyland](/wiki/Jim_Leyland "Jim Leyland"), who managed Dombrowski's 1997 World Series champion Florida Marlins, replaced Trammell as manager; two months later, in response to Troy Percival's arm problems, closer [Todd Jones](/wiki/Todd_Jones "Todd Jones"), who had spent five seasons in Detroit (1997–2001), signed a two-year deal to return to the Tigers.[[324]](#cite_note-327)[[325]](#cite_note-Rogers_and_Jones-328) Veteran left-hander [Kenny Rogers](/wiki/Kenny_Rogers_(baseball) "Kenny Rogers (baseball)") also joined the Tigers from the Texas Rangers in late 2005.[[325]](#cite_note-Rogers_and_Jones-328)

#### The return of the Tigers: 2006 American League Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: The return of the Tigers: 2006 American League Champions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Magglio_Ord%C3%B3%C3%B1ez_2008.jpg/250px-Magglio_Ord%C3%B3%C3%B1ez_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Magglio_Ord%C3%B3%C3%B1ez_2008.jpg)

[Magglio Ordóñez](/wiki/Magglio_Ord%C3%B3%C3%B1ez "Magglio Ordóñez") hit a walk-off home run to clinch the 2006 AL pennant

Main article: [2006 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2006_Detroit_Tigers_season "2006 Detroit Tigers season")

After years of futility, the 2006 season showed signs of hope. The impressive rookie campaigns of eventual AL Rookie of the Year [Justin Verlander](/wiki/Justin_Verlander "Justin Verlander"), centerfielder [Curtis Granderson](/wiki/Curtis_Granderson "Curtis Granderson"), and flamethrowing relief pitcher [Joel Zumaya](/wiki/Joel_Zumaya "Joel Zumaya"), coupled with a well-publicized early-season tirade by Leyland, helped the team explode and quickly rise to the top of the AL Central.[[131]](#cite_note-Anderson-133)[[278]](#cite_note-Leyland-280) The team reached a high point when they were 40 games over .500, but a second half swoon started to raise questions about the team's staying power.[[111]](#cite_note-Blown_lead-113)[[278]](#cite_note-Leyland-280) On August 27, a 7–1 victory over the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/2006_Cleveland_Indians_season "2006 Cleveland Indians season") gave the Tigers their 82nd victory and their first winning season since 1993.[[278]](#cite_note-Leyland-280)[[326]](#cite_note-329)[[327]](#cite_note-330) On September 24, the Tigers beat the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/2006_Kansas_City_Royals_season "2006 Kansas City Royals season") 11–4 to clinch their first playoff berth since 1987.[[328]](#cite_note-331)[[329]](#cite_note-332) A division title seemed inevitable. All that was required was one win in the final five games of the season, which included three games against the Royals, whom the Tigers had manhandled much of the season. However, the Tigers lost all five games to finish 95–67, and the division title went to the 96–66 [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/2006_Minnesota_Twins_season "2006 Minnesota Twins season"). The Tigers instead settled for the AL wild card.[[330]](#cite_note-333)

The playoffs saw the Tigers beat the heavily favored [New York Yankees](/wiki/2006_New_York_Yankees_season "2006 New York Yankees season") 3–1 in the [ALDS](/wiki/2006_American_League_Division_Series "2006 American League Division Series") and sweep the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/2006_Oakland_Athletics_season "2006 Oakland Athletics season") in the [2006 ALCS](/wiki/2006_American_League_Championship_Series "2006 American League Championship Series"), thanks to a walk-off home run in Game 4 by right fielder Magglio Ordóñez.[[331]](#cite_note-334)[[332]](#cite_note-335) They advanced to the [World Series](/wiki/2006_World_Series "2006 World Series"), where they lost to the underdog [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/2006_St._Louis_Cardinals_season "2006 St. Louis Cardinals season") in five games.[[333]](#cite_note-336)

### Falling short (2007–2010)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Falling short (2007–2010)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Curtis_Granderson.jpg/250px-Curtis_Granderson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Curtis_Granderson.jpg)

[Curtis Granderson](/wiki/Curtis_Granderson "Curtis Granderson") in 2007

#### 2007

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: 2007")]

Main article: [2007 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2007_Detroit_Tigers_season "2007 Detroit Tigers season")

During the offseason, the Tigers traded for outfielder [Gary Sheffield](/wiki/Gary_Sheffield "Gary Sheffield"), who had been a part of the 1997 World Series champion Florida Marlins managed by Jim Leyland.[[334]](#cite_note-337) In addition to acquisitions, Dombrowski developed a productive farm system. Justin Verlander and Joel Zumaya, the most notable rookie contributors to the 2006 team, were followed by [Andrew Miller](/wiki/Andrew_Miller_(baseball) "Andrew Miller (baseball)"), who was drafted in 2006 and called up early in the 2007 campaign, and minor leaguer [Cameron Maybin](/wiki/Cameron_Maybin "Cameron Maybin"), an athletic five-tool outfielder ranked #6 in [Baseball America](/wiki/Baseball_America "Baseball America")'s 2007 Top 100 Prospects.[[335]](#cite_note-338)

On June 12, Verlander threw the Tigers' first no-hitter since 1984 (Jack Morris) and the first in Comerica Park history, in a 4–0 win over the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/2007_Milwaukee_Brewers_season "2007 Milwaukee Brewers season").[[336]](#cite_note-339) The Tigers had the best record in baseball in late July, but lost a few players to injuries and started to play poorly in the second half.[[337]](#cite_note-340)[[338]](#cite_note-341)[[339]](#cite_note-342)[[340]](#cite_note-343) The Tigers were officially eliminated from playoff competition on September 26, 2007, when the [New York Yankees](/wiki/2007_New_York_Yankees_season "2007 New York Yankees season") clinched a wild card berth.[[341]](#cite_note-344)[[342]](#cite_note-345) The Tigers, at 88–74, finished second in the AL Central.[[343]](#cite_note-346)

Magglio Ordóñez captured the AL batting title in 2007 with a .363 average, becoming the first Tiger to win it since Norm Cash did so in 1961.[[344]](#cite_note-347)

#### 2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: 2008")]

Main article: [2008 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2008_Detroit_Tigers_season "2008 Detroit Tigers season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Justin_Verlander_2008.jpg/250px-Justin_Verlander_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Justin_Verlander_2008.jpg)

[Justin Verlander](/wiki/Justin_Verlander "Justin Verlander"), June 2008

Going into the 2008 season, the franchise traded for prominent talent in [Édgar Rentería](/wiki/%C3%89dgar_Renter%C3%ADa "Édgar Rentería") (from the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves")) and [Miguel Cabrera](/wiki/Miguel_Cabrera "Miguel Cabrera") and [Dontrelle Willis](/wiki/Dontrelle_Willis "Dontrelle Willis") (from the [Florida Marlins](/wiki/Florida_Marlins "Florida Marlins")).[[345]](#cite_note-348)[[346]](#cite_note-349) However, the Tigers, who now boasted the second-highest team payroll in the majors at over $138 million,[[347]](#cite_note-350) began the regular season by losing seven straight games.[[348]](#cite_note-351) The Tigers climbed back, and at the midway point of the season, they were 42–40.[[349]](#cite_note-352) In the end, the team finished miserably, slumping to a 74–88 record.[[350]](#cite_note-353) Justin Verlander finished with his worst season as a pro, as he went 11–17 with a 4.84 ERA.[[351]](#cite_note-354) The Tigers also lost closer Todd Jones to retirement on September 25, 2008.[[352]](#cite_note-355) Despite the disappointing season, the team set an attendance record in 2008, drawing 3,202,654 customers to Comerica Park.[[353]](#cite_note-356)

#### 2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: 2009")]

Main article: [2009 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2009_Detroit_Tigers_season "2009 Detroit Tigers season")

Going into the 2009 season, the Tigers acquired starter [Edwin Jackson](/wiki/Edwin_Jackson_(baseball) "Edwin Jackson (baseball)") from the 2008 AL Champion [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Rays "Tampa Bay Rays"), and called up rookie and former #1 draft pick [Rick Porcello](/wiki/Rick_Porcello "Rick Porcello").[[354]](#cite_note-357)[[355]](#cite_note-Porcello-358) Jackson was outstanding in the first half, making his first All-Star team, while Porcello was solid most of the year, posting a 14–9 record with a 3.96 ERA and displaying grit and maturity beyond his 20 years of age.[[355]](#cite_note-Porcello-358)[[356]](#cite_note-359) Justin Verlander bounced back from an off 2008 to win 19 games. He posted a 3.45 ERA and led the AL in strikeouts (269) to finish third in the AL Cy Young balloting.[[357]](#cite_note-2009_stats-360)[[358]](#cite_note-361)[[359]](#cite_note-362) [Fernando Rodney](/wiki/Fernando_Rodney "Fernando Rodney") assumed the closer role in spring training, replacing the retired Todd Jones.[[360]](#cite_note-363) Rodney responded with 37 saves in 38 tries, while [Bobby Seay](/wiki/Bobby_Seay "Bobby Seay"), [Fu-Te Ni](/wiki/Fu-Te_Ni "Fu-Te Ni"), [Brandon Lyon](/wiki/Brandon_Lyon "Brandon Lyon"), and [Ryan Perry](/wiki/Ryan_Perry "Ryan Perry") shored up the middle relief that plagued the team in 2007 and 2008.[[357]](#cite_note-2009_stats-360)[[361]](#cite_note-364)[[362]](#cite_note-365)[[363]](#cite_note-366)[[364]](#cite_note-367)

Despite the improvements, the Tigers once again found themselves struggling to hold a lead in the AL Central. The team entered September with a 7-game lead in the division, but wound up tied with the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/2009_Minnesota_Twins_season "2009 Minnesota Twins season") at 86 wins by the final day of the regular season.[[365]](#cite_note-368)[[366]](#cite_note-Tiebreaker-369) The season ended on October 6 with a 6–5 loss in 12 innings to the Twins in the [tie-breaker game](/wiki/2009_American_League_Central_tie-breaker_game "2009 American League Central tie-breaker game"), leaving the Tigers with an 86–77 record.[[366]](#cite_note-Tiebreaker-369)[[367]](#cite_note-370) The Tigers spent 146 days of the 2009 season in first place, but became the first team in Major League history to lose a three-game lead with four games left to play.[[366]](#cite_note-Tiebreaker-369)[[368]](#cite_note-371)

#### 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: 2010")]

Main article: [2010 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2010_Detroit_Tigers_season "2010 Detroit Tigers season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Alex_Avila_2010.jpg/250px-Alex_Avila_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alex_Avila_2010.jpg)

[Alex Avila](/wiki/Alex_Avila "Alex Avila"), March 2010

Entering 2010, the Tigers parted ways with Curtis Granderson and Edwin Jackson as part of a three-way trade with the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") and [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks"); in return they picked up outfield prospect [Austin Jackson](/wiki/Austin_Jackson_(baseball) "Austin Jackson (baseball)") and pitchers [Phil Coke](/wiki/Phil_Coke "Phil Coke"), Max Scherzer and [Daniel Schlereth](/wiki/Daniel_Schlereth "Daniel Schlereth").[[369]](#cite_note-372) Jackson made the Tigers opening day roster, and was American League Rookie of the Month for April.[[370]](#cite_note-373) 2010 also saw the debut of [Brennan Boesch](/wiki/Brennan_Boesch "Brennan Boesch"), who was named the AL Rookie of the Month for May and June.[[371]](#cite_note-374)[[372]](#cite_note-375)

At the All-Star break, the Tigers were a half-game out of first place in the AL Central, behind the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/2010_Chicago_White_Sox_season "2010 Chicago White Sox season").[[373]](#cite_note-376) However, a slow start after the break and injuries to three key players sent the Tigers into yet another second half tailspin.[[374]](#cite_note-377)[[375]](#cite_note-378)[[376]](#cite_note-2010_final_game-379) The Tigers finished the season in third place with an 81–81 record, 13 games back of the division-winning [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/2010_Minnesota_Twins_season "2010 Minnesota Twins season").[[376]](#cite_note-2010_final_game-379)[[377]](#cite_note-380) While playing outstanding baseball at home, the Tigers were just 29–52 on the road.[[378]](#cite_note-381)

Among the season highlights were Miguel Cabrera hitting .328 with 38 home runs and an AL-best 126 RBI, earning the AL [Silver Slugger Award](/wiki/Silver_Slugger_Award "Silver Slugger Award") at first base and finishing second in the AL MVP race (earning 5 of 28 first-place votes).[[379]](#cite_note-382)[[380]](#cite_note-383) Jackson (.293 average, 103 runs, 181 hits, 27 stolen bases) finished second in the AL Rookie of the Year voting.[[381]](#cite_note-2010_stats-384)[[382]](#cite_note-385) Justin Verlander enjoyed another strong season (18–9 record, 3.37 ERA, 219 strikeouts).[[381]](#cite_note-2010_stats-384)

##### The near-perfect game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: The near-perfect game")]

Main article: [Armando Galarraga's near-perfect game](/wiki/Armando_Galarraga%27s_near-perfect_game "Armando Galarraga's near-perfect game")

On June 2, 2010, [Armando Galarraga](/wiki/Armando_Galarraga "Armando Galarraga") was pitching a perfect game against the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians "Cleveland Indians") with 2 outs in the top of the ninth inning when first-base umpire [Jim Joyce](/wiki/Jim_Joyce "Jim Joyce") made a controversial call, ruling [Jason Donald](/wiki/Jason_Donald_(baseball) "Jason Donald (baseball)") safe at first. Video replay showed he was out.[[383]](#cite_note-Galarraga_MLive-386)[[384]](#cite_note-Galarraga_ESPN-387) A tearful Joyce later said, "I just cost that kid a perfect game. I thought he beat the throw. I was convinced he beat the throw, until I saw the replay."[[384]](#cite_note-Galarraga_ESPN-387) Galarraga would later tell reporters that Joyce apologized to him directly and gave him a hug.[[383]](#cite_note-Galarraga_MLive-386) The next day, with Joyce umpiring home plate, Galarraga brought out the lineup card and the two shook hands.[[385]](#cite_note-388) Despite nationwide support for overturning the call, which included supportive statements from the Governor of Michigan and the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), commissioner [Bud Selig](/wiki/Bud_Selig "Bud Selig") let the call stand. However, he said he would look into expanding [instant replay](/wiki/Instant_replay_in_baseball "Instant replay in baseball") in the future.[[386]](#cite_note-389)

### Division winners (2011–2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Division winners (2011–2014)")]

#### 2011: First AL Central Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: 2011: First AL Central Championship")]

Main article: [2011 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2011_Detroit_Tigers_season "2011 Detroit Tigers season")

The Tigers returned much of their roster from 2010, while adding relief pitcher [Joaquín Benoit](/wiki/Joaqu%C3%ADn_Benoit "Joaquín Benoit"), catcher/DH [Victor Martinez](/wiki/V%C3%ADctor_Mart%C3%ADnez_(baseball) "Víctor Martínez (baseball)"), and starting pitcher [Brad Penny](/wiki/Brad_Penny "Brad Penny").[[387]](#cite_note-390)[[388]](#cite_note-391)[[389]](#cite_note-392)

On May 7, Verlander took a perfect game against the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/2011_Toronto_Blue_Jays_season "2011 Toronto Blue Jays season") into the 8th inning. After a walk to [J. P. Arencibia](/wiki/J._P._Arencibia "J. P. Arencibia"), Verlander coaxed a double-play grounder and went on to the 9th inning to complete his second career no-hitter by facing the minimum 27 batters. It was the seventh no-hitter in Tigers history.[[390]](#cite_note-393) On August 27, Verlander defeated the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/2011_Minnesota_Twins_season "2011 Minnesota Twins season"), 6–4, to become the first Tiger since Bill Gullickson in 1991 to win 20 games in a season. Verlander also became the first major league pitcher since [Curt Schilling](/wiki/Curt_Schilling "Curt Schilling") in 2002 to reach 20 wins before the end of August.[[391]](#cite_note-394)

In May, the Tigers were as many as eight games back of the first place [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/2011_Cleveland_Indians_season "2011 Cleveland Indians season"). However, they would start to play better.[[392]](#cite_note-2011_division_CBS-395)[[393]](#cite_note-2011_division_MLive-396)

The Tigers sent five players to the 2011 All-Star Game.[[394]](#cite_note-2011_ASG_rosters-397) Catcher [Alex Avila](/wiki/Alex_Avila "Alex Avila") was voted in as a starter, while Justin Verlander, José Valverde and Miguel Cabrera were added as reserves.[[394]](#cite_note-2011_ASG_rosters-397) Verlander was unavailable to play in the All-Star Game due to the rule where starting pitchers who play the Sunday beforehand are ineligible.[[395]](#cite_note-398) Shortstop [Jhonny Peralta](/wiki/Jhonny_Peralta "Jhonny Peralta") was later added to the All-Star team when the Yankees' [Derek Jeter](/wiki/Derek_Jeter "Derek Jeter") was unable to play due to injury.[[394]](#cite_note-2011_ASG_rosters-397)[[396]](#cite_note-399)

As a three-way battle for the division title developed between the Tigers, Indians, and [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/2011_Chicago_White_Sox_season "2011 Chicago White Sox season"), the Tigers put together an 18–10 record in August to begin to pull away.[[393]](#cite_note-2011_division_MLive-396)[[397]](#cite_note-400) Starter [Doug Fister](/wiki/Doug_Fister "Doug Fister"), who was acquired at the trade deadline, provided an immediate spark, going 8–1 over the final two months of the season with a sparkling 1.79 ERA.[[398]](#cite_note-401)[[399]](#cite_note-402) After a loss on September 1, the Tigers reeled off a 12-game winning streak to put any thoughts of another late-season collapse to rest. The streak consisted of four consecutive three-game sweeps over their AL Central Division rivals. It was the Tigers' longest winning streak since the 1934 team won 14 straight.[[400]](#cite_note-403) On September 16, the Tigers clinched the AL Central Division title with a 3–1 win over the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/2011_Oakland_Athletics_season "2011 Oakland Athletics season"). It was their first AL Central title since joining the division in 1998, and first division title of any kind since 1987.[[392]](#cite_note-2011_division_CBS-395)[[393]](#cite_note-2011_division_MLive-396)

Members of the 2011 Tigers won multiple statistical awards in 2011. Verlander won the pitching triple crown, leading the AL in wins (24), ERA (2.40) and strikeouts (250). On November 15, Verlander was a unanimous selection for the AL Cy Young Award.[[401]](#cite_note-404) In a much closer vote six days later, Verlander also won AL MVP, becoming the first pitcher to do so since [Dennis Eckersley](/wiki/Dennis_Eckersley "Dennis Eckersley") in 1992.[[402]](#cite_note-405) Valverde was the AL saves leader with 49 (in 49 save opportunities), winning the 2011 [MLB Delivery Man of the Year Award](/wiki/MLB_Delivery_Man_of_the_Year_Award "MLB Delivery Man of the Year Award").[[205]](#cite_note-Valverde_delivery-207) Cabrera won the AL batting title with a .344 average, while also leading the AL in on-base percentage (.448) and doubles (48).[[403]](#cite_note-406)[[404]](#cite_note-407)

The Tigers beat the [New York Yankees](/wiki/2011_New_York_Yankees_season "2011 New York Yankees season") by a score of 3–2 in Game 5 of the [ALDS](/wiki/2011_American_League_Division_Series "2011 American League Division Series"), winning the series 3–2.[[405]](#cite_note-408) They advanced to the [ALCS](/wiki/2011_American_League_Championship_Series "2011 American League Championship Series"), but they lost to the defending AL Champion [Texas Rangers](/wiki/2011_Texas_Rangers_season "2011 Texas Rangers season"), 4–2.[[406]](#cite_note-409)

#### 2012: American League Champions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: 2012: American League Champions")]

Main article: [2012 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2012_Detroit_Tigers_season "2012 Detroit Tigers season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Miguel_Cabrera_%282011%29.jpg/250px-Miguel_Cabrera_%282011%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miguel_Cabrera_(2011).jpg)

In 2012, [Miguel Cabrera](/wiki/Miguel_Cabrera "Miguel Cabrera") became the first Major League player to win the [Triple Crown](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Triple_Crown "Major League Baseball Triple Crown") in 45 years.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Delmon_Young_and_Prince_Fielder_on_July_13%2C_2012.jpg/250px-Delmon_Young_and_Prince_Fielder_on_July_13%2C_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Delmon_Young_and_Prince_Fielder_on_July_13,_2012.jpg)

[Delmon Young](/wiki/Delmon_Young "Delmon Young") (left) and [Prince Fielder](/wiki/Prince_Fielder "Prince Fielder") (right) in 2012

In 2012, the Tigers looked to defend their 2011 AL Central Division title, with the hopes of earning a second consecutive playoff appearance. On January 24, the Tigers signed free agent All-Star first baseman [Prince Fielder](/wiki/Prince_Fielder "Prince Fielder") to a 9-year, $214 million contract.[[407]](#cite_note-410) The move came shortly after the Tigers learned that Víctor Martínez had torn his [anterior cruciate ligament](/wiki/Anterior_cruciate_ligament "Anterior cruciate ligament") during offseason training in [Lakeland, Florida](/wiki/Lakeland,_Florida "Lakeland, Florida"), and would likely miss the entire 2012 season.[[408]](#cite_note-411) Miguel Cabrera moved back to his original position of third base, leading to the eventual release of veteran [Brandon Inge](/wiki/Brandon_Inge "Brandon Inge") on April 26.[[409]](#cite_note-412) On July 23, the Tigers acquired veteran second baseman [Omar Infante](/wiki/Omar_Infante "Omar Infante"), who played for Detroit in 2003–07, and starting pitcher [Aníbal Sánchez](/wiki/An%C3%ADbal_S%C3%A1nchez "Aníbal Sánchez") from the [Miami Marlins](/wiki/Miami_Marlins "Miami Marlins") in exchange for starting pitcher [Jacob Turner](/wiki/Jacob_Turner "Jacob Turner") and two other minor leaguers.[[410]](#cite_note-413)

At the midway point of the 2012 season, the Tigers were three games under .500 (39–42).[[411]](#cite_note-414) The team played much better in the second half and, after a fierce battle down the stretch with the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/2012_Chicago_White_Sox_season "2012 Chicago White Sox season"), the Tigers clinched the AL Central division title on October 1 with a 6–3 win against the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/2012_Kansas_City_Royals_season "2012 Kansas City Royals season").[[412]](#cite_note-415) Coupled with the Tigers' division title in 2011, it marked the first back-to-back divisional titles in team history, and first back-to-back postseason appearances since 1934–35.[[413]](#cite_note-416) The Tigers concluded the season with an 88–74 record.[[414]](#cite_note-417)

On the final day of the season, Cabrera earned the Triple Crown in batting, leading the AL in batting average (.330), home runs (44), and runs batted in (139). No player had accomplished this feat since [Carl Yastrzemski](/wiki/Carl_Yastrzemski "Carl Yastrzemski") in 1967.[[415]](#cite_note-418) On the mound, starters Justin Verlander and Max Scherzer finished first and second among the American League strikeout leaders, with 239 and 231, respectively.[[416]](#cite_note-419) Verlander (17–8, 2.64 ERA) finished second in the Cy Young Award balloting to [David Price](/wiki/David_Price_(baseball) "David Price (baseball)") of the Tampa Bay Rays.[[417]](#cite_note-420)

In the [American League Division Series](/wiki/2012_American_League_Division_Series "2012 American League Division Series"), the Tigers defeated the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/2012_Oakland_Athletics_season "2012 Oakland Athletics season"), 3–2, earning their second straight trip to the [American League Championship Series](/wiki/2012_American_League_Championship_Series "2012 American League Championship Series").[[418]](#cite_note-421) The Tigers completed a four-game sweep of the [New York Yankees](/wiki/2012_New_York_Yankees_season "2012 New York Yankees season") in the ALCS to win their 11th AL pennant and earn a trip to the [World Series](/wiki/2012_World_Series "2012 World Series").[[419]](#cite_note-422) In the World Series, the Tigers were swept by the [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/2012_San_Francisco_Giants_season "2012 San Francisco Giants season").[[420]](#cite_note-423)

On November 15, 2012, Cabrera was named AL MVP.[[421]](#cite_note-424)

#### 2013: American League title defense

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: 2013: American League title defense")]

Main article: [2013 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2013_Detroit_Tigers_season "2013 Detroit Tigers season")

The Tigers entered the 2013 season looking to defend their 2012 AL pennant. Key acquisitions in the offseason included signing free agent outfielder [Torii Hunter](/wiki/Torii_Hunter "Torii Hunter") to a two-year, $26 million contract,[[422]](#cite_note-425) while also signing their 2012 trade deadline acquisition, pitcher Aníbal Sánchez, to a five-year, $80 million deal.[[423]](#cite_note-426) The Tigers also signed free agent catcher [Brayan Peña](/wiki/Brayan_Pe%C3%B1a "Brayan Peña") to a one-year contract.[[424]](#cite_note-december10-427) Moreover, ace starter Justin Verlander signed a $180 million contract extension.[[425]](#cite_note-428) The Tigers placed six players on the 2013 American League All-Star team: Miguel Cabrera, Prince Fielder, Jhonny Peralta, Torii Hunter, Max Scherzer and Justin Verlander.[[426]](#cite_note-429)

On September 25, the Tigers clinched their third consecutive AL Central Division title.[[427]](#cite_note-430) Tigers pitchers struck out 1,428 batters during the regular season, breaking the record of 1,404 held by the [2003 Chicago Cubs](/wiki/2003_Chicago_Cubs_season "2003 Chicago Cubs season").[[428]](#cite_note-431) Cabrera (.348 average, 44 HR, 139 RBI) was voted the AL MVP for the second straight season,[[429]](#cite_note-432) while Scherzer (21–3, 2.90 ERA, 240 strikeouts) won the AL Cy Young Award.[[430]](#cite_note-433)

The Tigers played the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/2013_Oakland_Athletics_season "2013 Oakland Athletics season") for the second straight year in the [ALDS](/wiki/2013_American_League_Division_Series "2013 American League Division Series"), and defeated the A's, 3–2.[[431]](#cite_note-434) The Tigers set a record by striking out 57 Oakland batters in the ALDS.[[432]](#cite_note-435) With his Game 5 gem, Verlander ran his postseason scoreless streak against Oakland to 30 innings.[[433]](#cite_note-436)

The Tigers advanced to their third straight [ALCS](/wiki/2013_American_League_Championship_Series "2013 American League Championship Series"), where they played the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/2013_Boston_Red_Sox_season "2013 Boston Red Sox season") for the first ever time in the postseason.[[434]](#cite_note-437) The Tigers would be defeated in six games.[[435]](#cite_note-438)

#### 2014: Changes at the top

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: 2014: Changes at the top")]

Main article: [2014 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2014_Detroit_Tigers_season "2014 Detroit Tigers season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Jim_Leyland_pregame_at_Dodger_Stadium.jpg/250px-Jim_Leyland_pregame_at_Dodger_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jim_Leyland_pregame_at_Dodger_Stadium.jpg)

[Jim Leyland](/wiki/Jim_Leyland "Jim Leyland"), manager from 2006 to 2013

Jim Leyland stepped down from his managerial position after eight years with Detroit,[[436]](#cite_note-439) and the Tigers hired [Brad Ausmus](/wiki/Brad_Ausmus "Brad Ausmus") as Leyland's successor.[[437]](#cite_note-440) On November 20, 2013, the Tigers traded Prince Fielder to the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") for three time All-Star second baseman [Ian Kinsler](/wiki/Ian_Kinsler "Ian Kinsler") plus cash considerations with regard to Fielder's remaining contract amount.[[438]](#cite_note-441) The Tigers later traded starting pitcher Doug Fister to the [Washington Nationals](/wiki/Washington_Nationals "Washington Nationals") for infielder [Steve Lombardozzi Jr.](/wiki/Steve_Lombardozzi_Jr. "Steve Lombardozzi Jr.") and pitchers [Ian Krol](/wiki/Ian_Krol "Ian Krol") and [Robbie Ray](/wiki/Robbie_Ray_(baseball) "Robbie Ray (baseball)").[[439]](#cite_note-442)

In an effort to improve a bullpen that often struggled in 2013, the Tigers signed veteran closer [Joe Nathan](/wiki/Joe_Nathan "Joe Nathan") to a two-year, $20 million contract, with a club option for 2016,[[440]](#cite_note-443) and later signed [Joba Chamberlain](/wiki/Joba_Chamberlain "Joba Chamberlain") to a one-year, $2.5 million deal.[[441]](#cite_note-444) On May 2, 2014, a month into the season, with the bullpen having a combined 5.37 ERA, which was 29th out of 30 in MLB, the Tigers signed free agent reliever [Joel Hanrahan](/wiki/Joel_Hanrahan "Joel Hanrahan") to a one-year contract.[[442]](#cite_note-445) However, he never came off the disabled list to pitch for the team.[[443]](#cite_note-446) The team further bolstered the bullpen near the MLB trading deadline, dealing pitchers [Corey Knebel](/wiki/Corey_Knebel "Corey Knebel") and [Jake Thompson](/wiki/Jake_Thompson "Jake Thompson") to the Texas Rangers in exchange for former All-Star closer [Joakim Soria](/wiki/Joakim_Soria "Joakim Soria") on July 23.[[444]](#cite_note-447)

On July 31, with just hours left before the end of the non-waiver trade deadline, the Tigers traded pitcher [Drew Smyly](/wiki/Drew_Smyly "Drew Smyly") and shortstop [Willy Adames](/wiki/Willy_Adames "Willy Adames") to the Tampa Bay Rays, and Austin Jackson to the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners") in a three-team deal to acquire pitcher David Price from the Rays.[[445]](#cite_note-448) With the acquisition of Price, the Tigers became the first team in major league history with three consecutive Cy Young Award winners in its starting rotation.[[446]](#cite_note-449)

On September 28, the last day of the regular season, Price pitched a 3–0 gem against the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/2014_Minnesota_Twins_season "2014 Minnesota Twins season"), and the Tigers clinched their fourth consecutive AL Central Division title.[[447]](#cite_note-450) The 90–72 Tigers finished one game ahead of the Kansas City Royals.[[448]](#cite_note-451)

The Tigers faced the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/2014_Baltimore_Orioles_season "2014 Baltimore Orioles season") in the [2014 American League Division Series](/wiki/2014_American_League_Division_Series "2014 American League Division Series"), where they were swept, 3–0.[[449]](#cite_note-452) [J. D. Martinez](/wiki/J._D._Martinez "J. D. Martinez") became the first player in franchise history to hit home runs in his first two career postseason games. Both were part of back-to-back homers, with Víctor Martínez and [Nick Castellanos](/wiki/Nick_Castellanos "Nick Castellanos") in Games 1 and 2, respectively.[[450]](#cite_note-453)

### 2015: Mid-season reboot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: 2015: Mid-season reboot")]

Main article: [2015 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2015_Detroit_Tigers_season "2015 Detroit Tigers season")

Brad Ausmus continued to manage the Tigers for a second season. Free agents Max Scherzer and Torii Hunter left for other teams at the end of the year, while Rick Porcello, [Eugenio Suárez](/wiki/Eugenio_Su%C3%A1rez "Eugenio Suárez"), Robbie Ray, and prospect [Devon Travis](/wiki/Devon_Travis "Devon Travis") were all lost through trades. On the receiving end, the Tigers traded for slugger [Yoenis Céspedes](/wiki/Yoenis_C%C3%A9spedes "Yoenis Céspedes"), relief pitcher [Alex Wilson](/wiki/Alex_Wilson_(baseball) "Alex Wilson (baseball)"), speedy outfielder [Anthony Gose](/wiki/Anthony_Gose "Anthony Gose") and starting pitchers [Alfredo Simón](/wiki/Alfredo_Sim%C3%B3n "Alfredo Simón") and [Shane Greene](/wiki/Shane_Greene "Shane Greene").[[451]](#cite_note-454)[[452]](#cite_note-455)[[453]](#cite_note-456)[[454]](#cite_note-457)[[455]](#cite_note-458)

After winning the first six games of the year in record-breaking fashion, the season slowly went downhill for the Tigers.[[456]](#cite_note-459) Inconsistent pitching, division rivals outperforming expectations, and injuries to multiple players, including Joe Nathan, who only appeared in one game, Victor Martínez, and career first stints on the disabled list for Justin Verlander and Miguel Cabrera, sent the team below the .500 mark as the trade deadline came and the decision was made to "reboot" the team.[[457]](#cite_note-460)[[458]](#cite_note-461) Within a two-day span in late July, the Tigers traded David Price, Joakim Soria and Cespedes, receiving six well regarded prospects in return, including [Daniel Norris](/wiki/Daniel_Norris_(baseball) "Daniel Norris (baseball)") and [Michael Fulmer](/wiki/Michael_Fulmer "Michael Fulmer").[[459]](#cite_note-462)

On August 4, longtime general manager Dave Dombrowski was released by the team, with assistant GM [Al Avila](/wiki/Al_Avila "Al Avila") being promoted to general manager and president of baseball operations.[[460]](#cite_note-463)

Despite difficulties, the Tigers still ended up sending four players to the [2015 MLB All-Star Game](/wiki/2015_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "2015 Major League Baseball All-Star Game"): Miguel Cabrera received his 10th career All-Star selection and the starting nod, but could not play due to injury; David Price received his 5th career selection as well as the credit for the win for the American League; and J.D. Martinez and Jose Iglesias both received their first career All-Star selections.[[461]](#cite_note-464)[[462]](#cite_note-465)

The Tigers ended the season in last place in the AL Central Division with a record of 74–87.[[463]](#cite_note-466) The pitching staff was one of the worst in MLB, ending 27th in ERA, 28th in FIP, and 27th in WHIP.[[464]](#cite_note-467) However, the team also ended with a team batting average of .270, the best in MLB,[[465]](#cite_note-468) while Miguel Cabrera finished with the highest player batting average in the AL and MLB (.338), earning his fourth batting title in five years.[[466]](#cite_note-469)

### 2016

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: 2016")]

Main article: [2016 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2016_Detroit_Tigers_season "2016 Detroit Tigers season")

Brad Ausmus and the Tigers entered the 2016 season with new pitching coach [Rich Dubee](/wiki/Rich_Dubee "Rich Dubee") and 9 out of 25 members of the 2015 roster being replaced through trades and free agency. Prominent additions included two highly sought free agents, starting pitcher [Jordan Zimmermann](/wiki/Jordan_Zimmermann "Jordan Zimmermann") and outfielder [Justin Upton](/wiki/Justin_Upton "Justin Upton"), as well as players acquired through trades: outfielder Cameron Maybin, and the veteran closer [Francisco Rodriguez](/wiki/Francisco_Rodr%C3%ADguez_(Venezuelan_pitcher) "Francisco Rodríguez (Venezuelan pitcher)"), who led a totally revamped bullpen.[[467]](#cite_note-470) The Tigers lost two 2016 draft picks due to free agent compensation but, because of their bottom-ten finish in 2015, they kept their first round pick.[[468]](#cite_note-471) Key veteran losses include catcher Alex Avila and outfielder [Rajai Davis](/wiki/Rajai_Davis "Rajai Davis"), who both signed free agent deals with other teams in the division.[[469]](#cite_note-472)[[470]](#cite_note-473)

The Tigers finished the season with a record of 86–75, eight games behind the first place [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/2016_Cleveland_Indians_season "2016 Cleveland Indians season").[[471]](#cite_note-474) Detroit was the final team to fall out of contention for a wild card spot, losing Saturday and Sunday games to the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/2016_Atlanta_Braves_season "2016 Atlanta Braves season"), while the two teams they were chasing, [Baltimore](/wiki/2016_Baltimore_Orioles_season "2016 Baltimore Orioles season") and [Toronto](/wiki/2016_Toronto_Blue_Jays_season "2016 Toronto Blue Jays season"), got needed wins.[[472]](#cite_note-475)[[473]](#cite_note-476)

Pitcher Michael Fulmer, acquired from the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") organization in 2015, won the 2016 AL Rookie of the Year Award.[[474]](#cite_note-477)

### Rebuilding (2017–2023)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Rebuilding (2017–2023)")]

#### 2017

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: 2017")]

Main article: [2017 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2017_Detroit_Tigers_season "2017 Detroit Tigers season")

Mike Ilitch, the Tigers owner since 1992, died at the age of 87 on February 10, 2017.[[475]](#cite_note-478) The team remains in an Ilitch family trust, under the leadership of Mike's son, [Christopher Ilitch](/wiki/Christopher_Ilitch "Christopher Ilitch").[[476]](#cite_note-ilitchowned-479)[[477]](#cite_note-480) Like the Detroit Red Wings, the Tigers honored their owner in multiple ways, the most prominent being a "Mr. I" uniform patch.[[478]](#cite_note-481)

After a disappointing record through the All-Star break, the Tigers began committing to a rebuild, trading J. D. Martinez,[[479]](#cite_note-482) Alex Avila and [Justin Wilson](/wiki/Justin_Wilson_(baseball) "Justin Wilson (baseball)") in July,[[480]](#cite_note-483) plus Justin Upton and Justin Verlander in August.[[481]](#cite_note-484)[[482]](#cite_note-485) On September 22, the Tigers announced that the team would not extend manager Brad Ausmus' contract past the 2017 season, ending his four-year tenure as manager. Under the management of Ausmus, the Tigers had a record of 314–332 (.486 winning percentage) and won one AL Central division title in 2014.[[483]](#cite_note-486) The Tigers went 6–24 in September, ending the season in a tie for the worst record in MLB with the [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/2017_San_Francisco_Giants_season "2017 San Francisco Giants season"). However, due to a tiebreaker, the Tigers were awarded the number one overall pick in the [2018 MLB draft](/wiki/2018_Major_League_Baseball_draft "2018 Major League Baseball draft").[[484]](#cite_note-487)

#### 2018

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: 2018")]

Main article: [2018 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2018_Detroit_Tigers_season "2018 Detroit Tigers season")

On October 20, 2017, the Tigers announced that [Ron Gardenhire](/wiki/Ron_Gardenhire "Ron Gardenhire") and the team had reached a three-year agreement for the former [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins") skipper to succeed Brad Ausmus as the team's manager.[[485]](#cite_note-488) While rebuilding with young players, the team also lost slugger Miguel Cabrera and expected ace pitcher Michael Fulmer to extensive stints on the disabled list.[[486]](#cite_note-489)[[487]](#cite_note-490) This led the team to the same 64–98 record as the previous year, the fifth worst record in MLB, but still good for third place in a very weak AL Central division.[[488]](#cite_note-491)[[489]](#cite_note-492)[[490]](#cite_note-493)

#### 2019

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: 2019")]

Main article: [2019 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2019_Detroit_Tigers_season "2019 Detroit Tigers season")

Significant changes to the 2019 season opening roster include the departure of [José Iglesias](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Iglesias_(baseball) "José Iglesias (baseball)"), and the one-year signings of the middle infield tandem of [Jordy Mercer](/wiki/Jordy_Mercer "Jordy Mercer") and [Josh Harrison](/wiki/Josh_Harrison "Josh Harrison"), both formerly of the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates").[[491]](#cite_note-494)[[492]](#cite_note-495)[[493]](#cite_note-496) On March 19, it was announced that Michael Fulmer would undergo Tommy John surgery and miss the 2019 season.[[494]](#cite_note-497) The Tigers finished the season with a 47–114 record, the worst in all of Major League Baseball, and their second worst season in franchise history after their 43–119 record in [2003](/wiki/2003_Detroit_Tigers_season "2003 Detroit Tigers season").[[495]](#cite_note-498) The 2019 Tigers tied the [1939 St. Louis Browns](/wiki/1939_St._Louis_Browns_season "1939 St. Louis Browns season") for the most home losses (59) during a season in the modern era.[[496]](#cite_note-499)

#### 2020

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: 2020")]

Main article: [2020 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2020_Detroit_Tigers_season "2020 Detroit Tigers season")

On April 6, Al Kaline died at the age of 85. Kaline had been affiliated with the team for 67 years, most recently as an executive.[[497]](#cite_note-500) The Tigers wore a No. 6 patch to honor him.[[498]](#cite_note-501) In the [shortened 60-game season](/wiki/2020_Major_League_Baseball_season#Revised_60-game_schedule "2020 Major League Baseball season") due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the Tigers remained in playoff contention heading into September.[[499]](#cite_note-502) On September 19, manager Ron Gardenhire announced his retirement from baseball effective immediately, citing health concerns. Bench coach [Lloyd McClendon](/wiki/Lloyd_McClendon "Lloyd McClendon") was named interim manager for the remainder of the season.[[500]](#cite_note-gardy-503) The Tigers ultimately faded down the stretch, finishing with a 23–35 record.[[501]](#cite_note-504)

#### 2021

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: 2021")]

Main article: [2021 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2021_Detroit_Tigers_season "2021 Detroit Tigers season")

On October 30, 2020, the Tigers hired [A. J. Hinch](/wiki/A._J._Hinch "A. J. Hinch") as manager.[[502]](#cite_note-505)[[503]](#cite_note-506) On May 18, 2021, [Spencer Turnbull](/wiki/Spencer_Turnbull "Spencer Turnbull") pitched the eighth no-hitter in Tigers history against the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners"). Turnbull became the first Tigers player to throw a no-hitter since Justin Verlander on May 7, 2011.[[504]](#cite_note-507) The Tigers finished the season with a 77–85 record.[[505]](#cite_note-508) This outperformed preseason predictions for the team, most of which said the Tigers would finish last.[[506]](#cite_note-509)[[507]](#cite_note-510)[[508]](#cite_note-511)

#### 2022

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: 2022")]

Main article: [2022 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2022_Detroit_Tigers_season "2022 Detroit Tigers season")

On August 10, 2022, the Tigers fired Al Avila as general manager.[[509]](#cite_note-512) On September 19, 2022, the Tigers hired [Scott Harris](/wiki/Scott_Harris_(baseball) "Scott Harris (baseball)") as president of baseball operations.[[510]](#cite_note-513) The Tigers finished the season with a 66–96 record and had one of the worst offenses in the league.[[511]](#cite_note-514) On October 25, 2022, the Tigers hired Rob Metzler as vice president and assistant general manager.[[512]](#cite_note-515)

#### 2023

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: 2023")]

Main article: [2023 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2023_Detroit_Tigers_season "2023 Detroit Tigers season")

On September 21, 2023, the Tigers hired [Jeff Greenberg](/wiki/Jeff_Greenberg_(sports_executive) "Jeff Greenberg (sports executive)") as general manager.[[513]](#cite_note-516) The Tigers finished the season with a 78–84 record, and in second place in the division.[[514]](#cite_note-517) This was the final season for long-time Tigers slugger [Miguel Cabrera](/wiki/Miguel_Cabrera "Miguel Cabrera"), who announced he would retire from playing. Following his retirement, he joined the Tigers' front office as a special assistant to [Scott Harris](/wiki/Scott_Harris_(baseball) "Scott Harris (baseball)").[[515]](#cite_note-518)

### Return to the playoffs (2024–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: Return to the playoffs (2024–present)")]

#### 2024

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=69 "Edit section: 2024")]

Main article: [2024 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2024_Detroit_Tigers_season "2024 Detroit Tigers season")

2024 was expected to be another quiet year for the Tigers. That appeared to be the case for much of the season as the team was 55–63 on August 10. The Tigers then went 31–11 to clinch a playoff berth for the first time since 2014 on September 27.[[516]](#cite_note-519) They finished the season with a 86–76 record.[[517]](#cite_note-520)

In the [American League Wild Card Series](/wiki/2024_American_League_Wild_Card_Series "2024 American League Wild Card Series"), the Tigers swept the [Houston Astros](/wiki/2024_Houston_Astros_season "2024 Houston Astros season") 2–0, securing their first postseason series win since 2013.[[518]](#cite_note-521) In the [American League Division Series](/wiki/2024_American_League_Division_Series "2024 American League Division Series"), the Tigers were defeated by the [Cleveland Guardians](/wiki/2024_Cleveland_Guardians_season "2024 Cleveland Guardians season") in five games.[[519]](#cite_note-2024_ALDS-522)

#### 2025

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: 2025")]

Main article: [2025 Detroit Tigers season](/wiki/2025_Detroit_Tigers_season "2025 Detroit Tigers season")

Despite a strong first half where they had the best overall record at 59–34 on July 8,[[520]](#cite_note-Schoenfield-523)[[521]](#cite_note-Stavenhagen-524) the Tigers faltered severely in the second half, going 28–37 after the [All-Star Game](/wiki/2025_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "2025 Major League Baseball All-Star Game"), including a 7–17 record in September.[[522]](#cite_note-525)[[523]](#cite_note-526) As a result, the [Cleveland Guardians](/wiki/2025_Cleveland_Guardians_season "2025 Cleveland Guardians season"), who were 15.5 games behind them on July 8 and 10.5 games back on September 1, won the AL Central Division title at 88–74.[[520]](#cite_note-Schoenfield-523)[[521]](#cite_note-Stavenhagen-524)[[524]](#cite_note-527)[[525]](#cite_note-528) Their collapse has been described as one of the worst in all of MLB history.[[520]](#cite_note-Schoenfield-523)[[521]](#cite_note-Stavenhagen-524)[[526]](#cite_note-529) Nevertheless, on September 27, the Tigers clinched a [postseason](/wiki/2025_Major_League_Baseball_postseason "2025 Major League Baseball postseason") berth for the second consecutive season with a 2–1 win over the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/2025_Boston_Red_Sox_season "2025 Boston Red Sox season").[[527]](#cite_note-530) They finished with the same 87–75 record as the [Houston Astros](/wiki/2025_Houston_Astros_season "2025 Houston Astros season"), but secured the last playoff spot via the head-to-head tiebreaker.[[528]](#cite_note-531)

The Tigers defeated the Guardians in the [American League Wild Card Series](/wiki/2025_American_League_Wild_Card_Series "2025 American League Wild Card Series"), 2–1.[[529]](#cite_note-2025_WC-532) In the [American League Division Series](/wiki/2025_American_League_Division_Series "2025 American League Division Series"), the Tigers were defeated by the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/2025_Seattle_Mariners_season "2025 Seattle Mariners season") in five games.[[530]](#cite_note-533)

## Nickname

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=73 "Edit section: Nickname")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Tiger_Stadium_exterior_April_2008_-_Detroit_Michigan.jpg/330px-Tiger_Stadium_exterior_April_2008_-_Detroit_Michigan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tiger_Stadium_exterior_April_2008_-_Detroit_Michigan.jpg)

[Tiger Stadium](/wiki/Tiger_Stadium_(Detroit) "Tiger Stadium (Detroit)"), home of the Tigers from 1912 to 1999, at the corner of [Michigan](/wiki/U.S._Route_12_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 12 in Michigan") and Trumbull Avenues in the [Corktown](/wiki/Corktown,_Detroit "Corktown, Detroit") district of Detroit

The team was referred to as the Wolverines and Creams in their inaugural season in 1894 before they became the Tigers.[[531]](#cite_note-534)[[532]](#cite_note-Mayer-535)[[533]](#cite_note-536) There are various legends about how they got their [nickname](/wiki/Nickname "Nickname").[[534]](#cite_note-Beck_nickname-537)[[535]](#cite_note-Bak-538) One involves the striped socks they wore.[[534]](#cite_note-Beck_nickname-537)[[535]](#cite_note-Bak-538)[[536]](#cite_note-Tigers_list-539) [George Stallings](/wiki/George_Stallings "George Stallings"), the team's manager, took credit for the name. However, the earliest known use of it appeared in the *[Detroit Free Press](/wiki/Detroit_Free_Press "Detroit Free Press")* on April 16, 1895, a year prior to Stallings joining the team.[[532]](#cite_note-Mayer-535)[[534]](#cite_note-Beck_nickname-537)[[535]](#cite_note-Bak-538)

In the book *A Place for Summer: A Narrative History of Tiger Stadium*, Richard Bak states that the name originated from the [Detroit Light Guard](/wiki/Detroit_Light_Guard "Detroit Light Guard") military unit, who were known as "The Tigers". They had played significant roles in certain [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") battles and in the 1898 [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War"). Upon entry into the majors, the ballclub sought and received formal permission from the Light Guard to use its [trademark](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark"). From that day forth, the team has been known as the Tigers.[[534]](#cite_note-Beck_nickname-537)[[535]](#cite_note-Bak-538)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=74 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### Chicago White Sox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=75 "Edit section: Chicago White Sox")]

The series between the Tigers and [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") is one of the oldest active rivalries in the league today. Both teams joined the American League in 1901 after being charter members of the original Western League. Both have actively played one another annually for over 120 seasons. As is often the case between professional sports teams located in Chicago or Detroit, there usually exists a rivalry as such with the [Bulls–Pistons rivalry](/wiki/Bulls%E2%80%93Pistons_rivalry "Bulls–Pistons rivalry") of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"), the [Blackhawks–Red Wings rivalry](/wiki/Blackhawks%E2%80%93Red_Wings_rivalry "Blackhawks–Red Wings rivalry") of the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"), and the [Bears–Lions rivalry](/wiki/Bears%E2%80%93Lions_rivalry "Bears–Lions rivalry") of the [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League").[[537]](#cite_note-540) Despite playing one another for over 2,200 games, both teams have yet to meet in the postseason.[[538]](#cite_note-541)[[539]](#cite_note-542)

### Divisional

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=76 "Edit section: Divisional")]

The Tigers also maintain divisional rivalries with the [Cleveland Guardians](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians "Cleveland Guardians"), the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals"), and the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins").[[540]](#cite_note-Rivalries_BYB-543)[[541]](#cite_note-544)[[542]](#cite_note-545) The rivalry with Cleveland came to a head when the Tigers played at [Progressive Field](/wiki/Progressive_Field "Progressive Field") on August 7, 2013, with the teams first and second in the AL Central standings. Many Tigers fans who made the short trip to Cleveland started several "Let's go Tigers!" chants while the game was tied in the 9th inning. Cleveland fans countered with a "Detroit's bankrupt!" chant, in reference to the [city's 2013 bankruptcy](/wiki/Detroit_bankruptcy "Detroit bankruptcy"). Footage of the chants from [SportsTime Ohio](/wiki/Bally_Sports_Great_Lakes "Bally Sports Great Lakes") circulated online, with many baseball fans on [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") criticizing Cleveland fans for the chant due to the circumstances of [Detroit's financial situation](/wiki/Decline_of_Detroit "Decline of Detroit").[[543]](#cite_note-546) The Tigers ended up defeating Cleveland 6–5 in 14 innings.[[544]](#cite_note-547) The teams met in the postseason for the first time in the 2024 American League Division Series, where the Guardians won the series 3–2.[[519]](#cite_note-2024_ALDS-522) They met again in the 2025 American League Wild Card Series, where the Tigers won the series 2–1.[[529]](#cite_note-2025_WC-532)

### Other teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=77 "Edit section: Other teams")]

The Tigers have a holdover rivalry with the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") due to when the Tigers competed in the AL East.[[545]](#cite_note-548)

Although the Tigers have had limited postseason matchups with their divisional rivals, they developed a significant playoff rivalry with the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") in 2006, 2011 and 2012, which saw the Tigers win each series,[[546]](#cite_note-549)[[547]](#cite_note-550)[[548]](#cite_note-551) including a four-game sweep in the 2012 ALCS.[[549]](#cite_note-552)

Additionally, the Tigers have had some rivalries with NL teams that they have faced repeatedly in the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), such as the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") (four times) and [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") (three times).[[550]](#cite_note-553)[[551]](#cite_note-554) In [interleague play](/wiki/Interleague_play "Interleague play"), the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") are the Tigers' "natural rival."[[552]](#cite_note-555)

## Fanbase

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=78 "Edit section: Fanbase")]

There are numerous Tigers fans along the [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") region throughout the state of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), northwestern [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), southwestern [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), as well as a small fan base in and around the [Erie, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Erie,_Pennsylvania "Erie, Pennsylvania") area, due in part to Detroit's proximity to these regions as well as the presence of the Tigers' [Double-A](/wiki/Double-A_(baseball) "Double-A (baseball)") affiliate [Erie SeaWolves](/wiki/Erie_SeaWolves "Erie SeaWolves") in northwestern Pennsylvania.[[553]](#cite_note-556)[[554]](#cite_note-Windsor_NYT-557)[[555]](#cite_note-Sarnia_fan-558)[[556]](#cite_note-559)[[557]](#cite_note-560) The Tigers have their [Triple-A](/wiki/Triple-A_(baseball) "Triple-A (baseball)") affiliate [Toledo Mud Hens](/wiki/Toledo_Mud_Hens "Toledo Mud Hens") in [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio") in addition to their Double-A affiliate in Erie.[[558]](#cite_note-561)

The Tigers continue to develop a strong and long line of baseball fans in Ontario; the majority of baseball fans in southwestern Ontario generally support the Tigers over the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays").[[554]](#cite_note-Windsor_NYT-557)[[555]](#cite_note-Sarnia_fan-558) The cities of [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor,_Ontario "Windsor, Ontario") and [Sarnia](/wiki/Sarnia "Sarnia"), Ontario, have large fan bases of loyal Tigers fans.[[554]](#cite_note-Windsor_NYT-557)[[555]](#cite_note-Sarnia_fan-558)
