## Rally cry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=81 "Edit section: Rally cry")]

During the 1968 season, the team was cheered on by the phrase, "Go Get 'Em, Tigers", which was made popular by a song of the same name written and recorded by [Artie Fields](/wiki/Artie_Fields "Artie Fields").[[560]](#cite_note-563) "Sock it to 'em, Tigers!" was also a popular phrase during this time.[[561]](#cite_note-564)[[562]](#cite_note-565)

During the 1984 World Series championship run, the team was cheered on to the cry, "Bless You Boys," a phrase coined by sportscaster Al Ackerman.[[224]](#cite_note-Ackerman_BYB-226)[[225]](#cite_note-Ackerman_COD-227)[[563]](#cite_note-566)

In 2005, the team began using the phrase "Who's Your Tiger?" as its slogan.[[564]](#cite_note-Zeringue-567)

During the 2006 season, a rally cry caught on in the Tigers' dugout. In a June game against the New York Yankees, Tigers pitcher [Nate Robertson](/wiki/Nate_Robertson "Nate Robertson") was featured in an in-game interview with [FSN Detroit](/wiki/Bally_Sports_Detroit "Bally Sports Detroit"). During the interview, Robertson began to stuff [Big League Chew](/wiki/Big_League_Chew "Big League Chew") bubble gum into his mouth. The Tigers would spark a rally, in which they came back to tie the game. As a result, the phrase "Gum Time" became popular.[[565]](#cite_note-568)[[566]](#cite_note-569)

Additionally, the chant of a local man, the late James Van Horn, who patrolled the streets around Comerica Park yelling out "Eat 'Em Up Tigers! Eat 'Em Up!", was very popular.[[567]](#cite_note-570)

In 2009, the team used the phrase "Always a Tiger" as its slogan.[[568]](#cite_note-571)

In 2011, the slogan was switched back to "Who's Your Tiger?"[[564]](#cite_note-Zeringue-567)

During the 2018 season, a rally cry caught on with both players and fans. In a May game against the [Los Angeles Angels](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels "Los Angeles Angels"), a goose appeared on the field during a second rain delay. After a few minutes, it left the field only to fly into the scoreboard. The goose was unharmed. When the game resumed, the Tigers scored five runs in the sixth inning and won the game, 6–1. As a result, the goose was referred to as the "Rally Goose" and the phrase became popular.[[569]](#cite_note-572)[[570]](#cite_note-573)

## Uniforms and logos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=82 "Edit section: Uniforms and logos")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Detroit-tigers-logo-2016.png/120px-Detroit-tigers-logo-2016.png)](/wiki/File:Detroit-tigers-logo-2016.png)

*Primary logo  
2016–present*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Detroit_Tigers_Insignia.svg/120px-Detroit_Tigers_Insignia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Detroit_Tigers_Insignia.svg)

*Cap logo  
1924–present. It is orange for road games.*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Detroit_tigers_textlogo.svg/120px-Detroit_tigers_textlogo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Detroit_tigers_textlogo.svg)

Logo used on the home jersey until 2018. It was also the primary logo prior to 2016.

See also: [Major League Baseball § Uniforms](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball#Uniforms "Major League Baseball")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Ron_LeFlore_1975.jpg/250px-Ron_LeFlore_1975.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ron_LeFlore_1975.jpg)

Outfielder [Ron LeFlore](/wiki/Ron_LeFlore "Ron LeFlore") wearing the traditional Tigers home uniform with navy blue piping down the front and an Old English "D" on the left chest

The Tigers have worn essentially the same home [uniform](/wiki/Baseball_uniform "Baseball uniform") since 1934 — solid white [jersey](/wiki/Jersey_(sport) "Jersey (sport)") with navy blue [piping](/wiki/Piping_(sewing) "Piping (sewing)") down the front and an Old English "D" on the left chest, white pants, navy blue [hat](/wiki/Baseball_cap "Baseball cap") with a white letter D in the [blackletter](/wiki/Blackletter "Blackletter") or *textur*/*textualis* typeface associated with [Middle](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") and [Early Modern English](/wiki/Early_Modern_English "Early Modern English") and popularly referred to as "Old English" even though it was not used for [that language](/wiki/Old_English "Old English").[[571]](#cite_note-Unified_logo-574)[[572]](#cite_note-Jersey_USAT-575) On the Tigers' road uniforms, the D on their hats is orange and a script "Detroit" appears across the jersey. The script "Detroit" design was first used as part of their road jersey in 1930, and would remain so until 1959, when they changed it to a block lettering style.[[573]](#cite_note-TigersUniforms1-576)

The current version of the Tigers' road uniform was first worn in 1994, initially with racing stripes, before going with a stripe-free design from 1995 to 1997, and then to the current version featuring navy piping inspired by the team's mid-1950s road uniforms. Prior to readopting the orange "D" all-navy road cap with this uniform, the Tigers wore two different cap variations with orange brim: one with just the orange "D" which they only wore in 1994, and another featuring a tiger walking through a white "D" logo trimmed in orange, which they wore until 1997.[[573]](#cite_note-TigersUniforms1-576)[[574]](#cite_note-TigersUniforms2-577)

The Tigers previously sported road uniforms with a block "Detroit" lettering from 1960 to 1993, adding orange trim in 1972. The caps initially featured white trim within the orange "D" until 1982.[[573]](#cite_note-TigersUniforms1-576)[[574]](#cite_note-TigersUniforms2-577) A version of the team's blackletter D was first seen on Tigers uniforms in 1904, after using a simple block D in 1903. The blackletter D appeared frequently after that until being established in 1934.[[571]](#cite_note-Unified_logo-574)[[572]](#cite_note-Jersey_USAT-575) In 1960, the Tigers changed their uniform to read "Tigers" in script form, but the change only lasted one season before the traditional uniform was reinstated.[[572]](#cite_note-Jersey_USAT-575)[[575]](#cite_note-578)

The Tigers used to have different versions of the Old English D on the cap and jersey.[[571]](#cite_note-Unified_logo-574)[[576]](#cite_note-579) In 2018, the Tigers changed the classic curved logo on their home uniforms to match that of the sharp-cornered hat logo; additionally, the logo on the cap was enlarged.[[571]](#cite_note-Unified_logo-574)[[577]](#cite_note-580)[[578]](#cite_note-581) In 2019, the Tigers reverted to the smaller logo on the cap, but kept the cap sharp-cornered D on the home jerseys.[[579]](#cite_note-582)

In 2023, the Tigers entered into a uniform sponsorship deal with [Meijer](/wiki/Meijer "Meijer"), the state's major [supercenter](/wiki/Supercenter "Supercenter") chain based in [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan") and a longtime team sponsor. The uniform patch's logo (attached to the sleeve of the player's dominant hand) is rendered solely in navy blue on white for the home uniform, and white text on a black background for the road jersey with an orange outline.[[580]](#cite_note-583)

**Unique characteristics of the Tigers uniforms:**

* The Tigers are the only team in Major League Baseball to have a color on their [road](/wiki/Road_(sports) "Road (sports)") uniforms that is not on their home uniforms (orange). The Tigers first added orange to their road uniform in 1903, but it was not until 1972 that the Tigers made orange an accent color on their road uniforms full-time. The Tigers also wore the "orange D" navy caps at home during the late 1940s through the 1950s.[[581]](#cite_note-584)[[573]](#cite_note-TigersUniforms1-576)[[574]](#cite_note-TigersUniforms2-577)
* The Tigers' uniforms have more belt loops than those of any other team, as their uniform pants do not feature the wide "tunnel" loops that appear on most baseball pants.[[582]](#cite_note-585)

**Alternate jerseys:**

In 1995, the Tigers introduced a solid navy blue [alternate jersey](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey"), which featured the team's primary [logo](/wiki/Logo "Logo") at the time, a tiger stepping through the D, on the chest. It was worn for one home game.[[583]](#cite_note-586)

The Tigers wear the throwback jerseys of the [Detroit Stars](/wiki/Detroit_Stars "Detroit Stars") for their annual Negro Leagues Tribute Game in order to pay tribute to the [Negro leagues](/wiki/Negro_leagues "Negro leagues") players and their contributions to the game of baseball.[[584]](#cite_note-587)

The Tigers wear a white and navy blue home jersey with "Tigres" across the chest for their annual "¡Fiesta Tigres!" game to recognize and honor the contributions of Hispanic and Latino players and coaches to the game of baseball.[[585]](#cite_note-588)

Like all of MLB, the Tigers wore a highly stylized and brightly colored jersey for [Players Weekend](/wiki/Players_Weekend "Players Weekend"). In the inaugural games from August 25–27, 2017, their away jerseys were grey with bright orange with "Tigers" on the chest, the orange cap had a tiger instead of the Old English "D" on it. Players were also encouraged to use nicknames on the back of their jerseys.[[586]](#cite_note-589) In the 2018 season, the Tigers wore a blue jersey with orange sleeves that said "Tigers" on the chest, with an orange cap that had a tiger on it.[[587]](#cite_note-590)

In 2024, the Tigers unveiled their first [City Connect](/wiki/City_Connect "City Connect") jersey, which is a nod to Detroit's automobile industry. The primarily navy blue jersey with electric blue tire tread patterns has the "Motor City" nickname emblazoned in front along with racing stripes on the sleeves and pants. The sleeve patch features the diamond [highway shield](/wiki/Highway_shield "Highway shield") for the [Michigan State Trunkline Highway System](/wiki/Michigan_State_Trunkline_Highway_System "Michigan State Trunkline Highway System") and alludes to Comerica Park's location on Woodward Avenue, otherwise known as [M-1](/wiki/M-1_(Michigan_highway) "M-1 (Michigan highway)"), with the team's "D" logo replacing the "block M"; when shown in a different light, it shows Detroit's [313 area code](/wiki/313_area_code "313 area code"), with the 3's rendered in a reflective text. Along the jock tag is the illustration of a tiger's eyes below the team name. The cap is navy blue with "Detroit" emblazoned in front, and the right side features a faux [vehicle identification number](/wiki/Vehicle_identification_number "Vehicle identification number") tag, "*190135456884*", referencing the Tigers' first American League season of 1901 and its four World Series championships mentioned by the year in two digits.[[588]](#cite_note-591)

In 2026, the Tigers unveiled two new alternative uniforms: an orange home alternate and a navy road alternate. Both uniforms draw inspiration from the striping and logos used during the 1970s, 1980s, and early 1990s, including the 1984 World Series championship team. The orange jersey features the Old English D on the left breast in navy, outlined in white. The navy jersey displays the Detroit script—present on the team's existing road uniforms—in orange, double-outlined in navy and white. Additionally, a new cap was introduced to be worn with both uniforms, featuring a navy crown, an orange Old English D, and an orange bill.[[589]](#cite_note-592)

* [Ty Cobb](/wiki/Ty_Cobb "Ty Cobb"), [Harry Heilmann](/wiki/Harry_Heilmann "Harry Heilmann"), [Heinie Manush](/wiki/Heinie_Manush "Heinie Manush"), [Hughie Jennings](/wiki/Hughie_Jennings "Hughie Jennings"), and [Sam Crawford](/wiki/Sam_Crawford "Sam Crawford") are honored by their names on the wall at Comerica Park. They all played in an era where numbers were not worn on jerseys.[[622]](#cite_note-Trammell's_number-625)[[625]](#cite_note-Numbers_DAC-628)[[605]](#cite_note-Heilmann_HOF-608)[[611]](#cite_note-Manush_HOF-614)[[608]](#cite_note-Jennings_HOF-611)[[596]](#cite_note-Crawford_HOF-599)
* Though their numbers are not officially retired, the names of [Mickey Cochrane](/wiki/Mickey_Cochrane "Mickey Cochrane") and [George Kell](/wiki/George_Kell "George Kell") are displayed at Comerica Park to honor their contributions to the Detroit Tigers organization. Cochrane wore No. 3, later retired for [Alan Trammell](/wiki/Alan_Trammell "Alan Trammell"), and Kell wore No. 21 for five years, 7 for two years, and 15 for one year.[[625]](#cite_note-Numbers_DAC-628) They both entered the [National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") as Detroit Tigers, and their plaques in the Hall show them wearing the Tigers' cap.[[595]](#cite_note-Cochrane_HOF-598)[[609]](#cite_note-Kell_HOF-612)
* [Al Kaline](/wiki/Al_Kaline "Al Kaline") was also a TV color commentator for the Tigers from 1975 to 2002.[[626]](#cite_note-629) George Kell was also a play-by-play announcer for the Tigers from 1959 to 1963 and again from 1965 to 1996.[[609]](#cite_note-Kell_HOF-612)
* [Ernie Harwell](/wiki/Ernie_Harwell "Ernie Harwell") spent 42 years (in two stints) calling Tigers games on radio and television.[[627]](#cite_note-630) Harwell, Kell and the aforementioned five players honored by name, are stacked together in right field.[[628]](#cite_note-631)
* [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson")'s No. **42** was retired throughout [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") in 1997.[[621]](#cite_note-Retired_numbers-624)

Almost all the players with retired numbers (and Ty Cobb) also have statues of themselves that sit behind their names, which are painted on the left-center field wall.[[629]](#cite_note-632)

National Avenue, which runs behind the third-base stands at the Tigers' previous home Tiger Stadium, was renamed Cochrane Street for Mickey Cochrane.[[630]](#cite_note-633) Cherry Street, which runs behind the left-field stands at Tiger Stadium, was renamed Kaline Drive for Al Kaline.[[631]](#cite_note-634)

The most recent number retired by the Tigers was [Jim Leyland](/wiki/Jim_Leyland "Jim Leyland"), who had his No. 10 retired on August 3, 2024.[[624]](#cite_note-Leyland_DN-627)

## Broadcasters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=91 "Edit section: Broadcasters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Ernie_Harwell_-_WJROneOfAKind.jpg/250px-Ernie_Harwell_-_WJROneOfAKind.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ernie_Harwell_-_WJROneOfAKind.jpg)

[Ernie Harwell](/wiki/Ernie_Harwell "Ernie Harwell") served as a broadcaster for the Tigers from 1960 to 1991, 1993 to 2002

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=92 "Edit section: Radio")]

Main article: [Detroit Tigers Radio Network](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers_Radio_Network "Detroit Tigers Radio Network")

The Tigers' current [flagship radio station](/wiki/Flagship_(radio) "Flagship (radio)") is [WXYT-FM](/wiki/WXYT-FM "WXYT-FM") (97.1 FM).[[638]](#cite_note-Radio_affiliates-641) [Dan Dickerson](/wiki/Dan_Dickerson "Dan Dickerson") does [play-by-play](/wiki/Play-by-play "Play-by-play") with [Bobby Scales](/wiki/Bobby_Scales "Bobby Scales"), [Dan Petry](/wiki/Dan_Petry "Dan Petry"), and [Andy Dirks](/wiki/Andy_Dirks "Andy Dirks") rotating on [color commentary](/wiki/Color_commentary "Color commentary"). On games where Dickerson is assigned to work on television, Greg Gania serves as an alternate.[[639]](#cite_note-2025_broadcasters-642)[[640]](#cite_note-643)[[641]](#cite_note-644) Games are [syndicated](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers_Radio_Network "Detroit Tigers Radio Network") throughout Michigan, [Toledo](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio") and [Archbold, Ohio](/wiki/Archbold,_Ohio "Archbold, Ohio").[[638]](#cite_note-Radio_affiliates-641)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Detroit_Tigers&action=edit&section=93 "Edit section: Television")]

In February 2026, Ilitch Sports + Entertainment announced that the Tigers regional broadcasts would be distributed by [MLB Local Media](/wiki/MLB_Local_Media "MLB Local Media") beginning in the 2026 season. The agreement also included the co-owned [Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings").[[642]](#cite_note-645) The next month, Ilitch Sports + Entertainment announced the creation of [Detroit SportsNet](/wiki/Detroit_SportsNet "Detroit SportsNet") to continue syndicating games to cable and satellite providers in the Tigers' regional market.[[643]](#cite_note-646)

The Tigers' television broadcast team consists of [Jason Benetti](/wiki/Jason_Benetti "Jason Benetti") on play-by-play with Andy Dirks, Dan Petry, [Todd Jones](/wiki/Todd_Jones "Todd Jones"), and Bobby Scales rotating on color commentary. On games where Benetti is assigned to work for [NBC Sports](/wiki/NBC_Sports "NBC Sports"), Dan Dickerson serves as an alternate.[[639]](#cite_note-2025_broadcasters-642)[[644]](#cite_note-647)[[645]](#cite_note-648)
